generated_summary: Voters in 11 states are set to elect governors tonight, with Republicans on track to increase their count from 29 to at least 30, potentially securing more than two-thirds of the nation's top state offices. Of the 11 seats up for grabs, eight are currently held by Democrats and three by Republicans. Analysts identify only three contests as truly competitive: Montana, New Hampshire, and Washington, all of which feature non-incumbent Democratic candidates. In Montana, Democratic Attorney General Steve Bullock faces GOP Rep. Rick Hill; in New Hampshire, Maggie Hassan faces Tea Party conservative Ovide Lamontagne; and in Washington, former Rep. Jay Inslee is in a dead-heat with Rob McKenna. Republicans are expected to flip the North Carolina governorship from Democratic control, as Democratic Gov. Beverly Perdue declined to seek re-election, leaving Republican Pat McCrory favored to win. Republicans are also projected to win seats in Utah, North Dakota, and Indiana, with Jack Dalrymple, Gary Herbert, and Mike Pence expected to secure safe wins. Democrats are expected to maintain their seats in West Virginia, Missouri, Vermont, and Delaware, with Peter Shumlin and Jack Markell expected to notch safe wins. Beyond the election results, the role of governors is expected to gain significant influence regarding health care. Political scientist Thad Kousser notes that a recent U.S. Supreme Court decision allowing states to opt out of the Affordable Care Act's Medicaid expansion confers "incredible power" on governors. Kousser suggests a Mitt Romney presidency would further empower these officials.

Facebook admitted to erroneously removing a photo of two men kissing from a page promoting a "gay kiss-in" demonstration in London, an event organized to protest a local pub's decision to eject a same-sex couple for kissing. Despite Facebook’s initial claim that the image violated policies regarding nudity or sexually suggestive content, a company representative confirmed the removal was an error and apologized. The removal sparked a wave of online protest, with dozens of users posting their own photos of same-sex couples kissing in response. Richard Metzger, who became an accidental spokesperson for the issue, clarified that the photo used to illustrate his first post about the John Snow Kiss-In was a promotional still from the British soap opera "Eastenders." The image features the character Christian Clarke, played by actor John Partridge. This specific scene had previously caused scandal in the UK in October 2008, leading to numerous complaints to the BBC because it was broadcast before the watershed. Clarifications were also issued regarding the organization of the event; media reports had incorrectly attributed the London "Kiss-In" to Metzger, who resides in Los Angeles. The event was actually organized by Paul Shetler and local organizers. Furthermore, the Facebook event page was not blocked by the platform; Shetler made the page private after the event concluded to shield it from trolls.

On Tuesday, the Siskiyou County Board of Supervisors voted 4-1 to pursue seceding from the state of California, marking the first step in a long series of actions to form the proposed state of Jefferson. The vote occurred in front of more than 100 people who filled the supervisors' chambers, many of whom expressed support for the declaration. Proponents, led by rancher Mark Baird, argue that the move would bring representation to rural Northern California and Southern Oregon counties that are currently beholden to the whims of representatives from more heavily populated Southern California and free them from burdensome state regulations. The movement is driven by a growing dissatisfaction with the political influence of Sacramento and the regulatory environment of the state capital. Residents have lobbied the board over a laundry list of complaints, including a lack of representation for the Republican-majority county, issues pertaining to water rights, and the rural fire prevention fee. During the discussion, many attendees argued that current state regulations are unconstitutional and infringe upon their rights. While most supervisors supported the move, Board Chair Ed Valenzuela was the sole dissenting vote, stating he is bound by his oath to uphold the Constitution of the state of California. To achieve statehood, the proposal must eventually be approved by the state legislature and the U.S. Congress.

Microsoft's acquisition of Nokia’s cell phone business and its patent portfolio for 5.44 billion euros is aimed at building a devices and services strategy, accelerating the company's transition away from the moribund world of the beige desktop toward the sunlit world of smartphones and tablets. This move marks a shift toward vertical integration, seeking to create a cohesive experience across Microsoft's diverse portfolio, including Windows, Xbox, Surface, and Bing. By acquiring Nokia, Microsoft secures a significant patent portfolio and a foothold in emerging markets, aiming to prevent competitors like Apple and Google from foreclosing app innovation, integration, and distribution. Nokia lashed itself to Microsoft's mast after losing significant market share—dropping from over 35% in 2003 to a struggling position against iOS and Android. The acquisition is seen by many analysts as an inevitable move for survival, providing an opportunity for Microsoft to step up its business mobility efforts and capture the enterprise mobile space, filling a void left by BlackBerry. While the deal's valuation is considered reasonable compared to the $8.5 billion paid for Skype in 2011, the primary challenge remains winning over consumers in mature markets. Microsoft must balance its PC heritage with new hardware ambitions, utilizing Nokia’s Asha devices as an "on-ramp" for consumers while attempting to unite its disparate brands into a unified consumer experience.

The U.S. Supreme Court begins a new term facing a high-stakes docket of politically charged cases that are expected to shape the political debate during the final stretch of the 2016 presidential campaign. A primary focus is the constitutionality of the University of Texas at Austin's affirmative action program, which the justices will revisit after previously punting the case to an appeals court. The court is also set to hear arguments regarding the death penalty, with at least six capital-punishment cases on the docket, including those concerning capital sentences in Florida, Georgia, Kansas, and Pennsylvania. The justices will also adjudicate several petitions from religious institutions, including the Little Sisters of the Poor, seeking exemptions from Obamacare’s contraception requirements. Regarding labor, the court is reviewing cases like Friedrichs v. California Teachers Association to determine if states can compel government employees to pay union dues. While the court previously ruled in Abood that nonmembers cannot be forced to pay for purely political activities, conservative lawyers hope to weaken government employee unions. Additionally, the court may address Evenwel v. Abbott regarding the apportionment of state legislative districts and the Obama administration's executive actions on granting quasi-legal status and work permits to millions of immigrants. With the current court split along partisan lines, observers expect a return to the norm where the right side of the court wins a majority of the big cases, with Justice Kennedy remaining a key vote.

Preservationist Adam Woodward has discovered what he believes to be the 18th-century foundation of the legendary Bull’s Head Tavern in the basement of 50 Bowery in Manhattan. Woodward documented ancient, hand-hewn and hand-planed wood joists and stone foundation walls, which he suspects are structural elements of the Colonial-era tavern used in the construction of the much larger Atlantic Garden beer hall. Historian David Freeland suggests that if confirmed, these remnants would make the site the oldest surviving structure in Manhattan. The Bull’s Head Tavern opened around 1750 and gained historical significance in 1783 when George Washington and Governor George Clinton stopped there after the English retreated from the city. While Woodward and Freeland are urging the preservation of the site for archaeological record, the current owner, Alex Chu, is moving forward with the demolition of the building to make way for a new hotel. Although the Landmarks Preservation Commission is aware of the discovery, they lack the jurisdiction to mandate an archaeological survey, though they can provide a list of experts to the owner. Woodward hopes the demolition can be delayed to record the site’s history before it is permanently destroyed.

The incursion of an unmanned aircraft into Israeli airspace over the weekend exposed perceived weaknesses in Israel's air defenses, according to an Iranian military official. The Israeli air force shot down a drone on Saturday after it crossed into southern Israel, having been first spotted above the Mediterranean near the Hamas-ruled Gaza Strip. While the Pentagon previously noted that Israel intercepted more than 80 percent of nearly 300 rockets and mortars fired at southern Israel in March, Iranian officials have criticized the effectiveness of the Iron Dome system, claiming it is designed for short-range rockets rather than slow-flying aircraft. Simultaneously, tensions in the Gaza Strip escalated following an Israeli air strike that killed one militant and wounded a second a day earlier. In response, the armed wing of Hamas and the Islamic Jihad group launched a coordinated rocket barrage toward southern Israel. This marked a significant escalation, as Hamas spokesman Abu Ubaida stated the movement would not remain passive in the face of "one-sided" Israeli violence. The Israeli army responded by targeting "Hamas terror activity sites" and using tank fire near Rafah, which wounded four children and damaged local infrastructure. While some officials link such aerial incursions to Hezbollah and the Iranian government, the ongoing friction highlights a volatile border situation where frequent clashes and the threat of broader regional conflict remain constant.

U.S. Federal prosecutors in Brooklyn filed papers on Thursday to forfeit a multimillion-dollar 1911 cubist painting by Pablo Picasso, titled "La Coiffeuse" (The Hairdresser), which was stolen from a storeroom at the Centre Georges Pompidou in Paris in 2001. The century-old canvas, valued at over $2.5 million, was missing for more than a decade before being unearthed in December in a FedEx shipment from Belgium to the Port of Newark. To evade detection, the painting was shipped under false pretenses as a $37 holiday-themed "art craft" or "toy" with "Joyeux Noel" written on the shipping papers. The work, which was originally bequeathed to the National Museums of France and last seen in Munich in 1998, was eventually tracked to a climate-controlled storage facility in Long Island City, Queens. Attorney Loretta Lynch filed a civil complaint to forfeit the painting and return it to France. French officials visited New York last month to confirm the painting's identity against historical records. The Pompidou Centre in Paris plans to display the recovered artwork publicly in May. Pompidou director Alain Seban expressed relief over the recovery, noting that the return of such cultural artifacts provides a sense of comfort following the destruction of Iraqi heritage by the Islamic State. While federal officials have not announced any arrests, Homeland Security Investigations noted that the market for selling stolen antiquities in the United States is increasingly drying up.

Since 1996, Alexa Internet has been donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive, where the information is added to the Wayback Machine following a specific embargo period. In regional news, the WCCO Viewers' Choice for Best Egg Roll in Minnesota named Que Viet, a popular neighborhood restaurant in northeast Minneapolis, as the winner. Other culinary highlights include a list of the five best distilleries in Minneapolis to help find the best spots to meet specific needs, a top-rated burger in St. Paul, and the unique "BB" Boulevardier—a bourbon-based cocktail riff on the classic Boulevardier—served at Mike's Mix. Notable cultural and regional landmarks include the Sunshine Factory in New Hope, which opened in 1976 and is noted for its 1970s aesthetic, and three artsy deals worth seeking out in St. Paul.

Officer Rogelio Santander, Badge #934, died Wednesday morning at Texas Health Presbyterian Dallas hospital less than 24 hours after being shot in the back of the head during a shooting at a North Dallas Home Depot store on Tuesday, April 24, 2018. Dallas Mayor Mike Rawlings and Police Chief U. Renee Hall announced the death during a city council meeting, noting that Santander had served with the department for three years. The shooting occurred just after 4 p.m. at the store located in the 11600 block of Forest Central Drive. Officers Santander and Crystal Almeida, 26, responded to a call regarding a suspicious male, Armando Luis Juarez, who was being detained by an off-duty officer. While the officers were in the store’s loss-prevention office, Juarez opened fire, striking Santander, Almeida, and Home Depot loss-prevention officer Scott Painter. Following the shooting, police launched a massive manhunt. Juarez fled the scene in a white work truck and was eventually apprehended in a residential neighborhood near Love Field Airport. Juarez, 29, was initially charged with aggravated assault against a public servant and a civilian; following Santander's death, a capital murder charge was added. Juarez is currently held on a $1.1 million bond as the district attorney's office prepares for prosecution. While Santander succumbed to his injuries, Almeida and Painter remain in critical condition but are making "remarkable recoveries" following surgery.

In a press release published Friday, the U.S. Coast Guard announced it is searching for a man who has cost the service approximately $500,000 by making nearly 30 false distress alerts starting in July 2014. The hoax calls originated from around the area of Annapolis, Maryland, specifically between Loretta Heights and Admiral Drive, and were directed toward emergency radio channels to broadcast the word "mayday." The two most recent incidents occurred on the night of July 21 and the morning of July 22. Cmdr. Sara Wallace, who heads the response sector in Maryland, emphasized that such hoaxes are deadly offenses that significantly distract rescuers from real emergencies, endangering both the public and responding crews. To assist in the investigation, the Coast Guard has released an audio file of the caller's voice. Making false distress calls is a felony punishable by up to six years in prison, a $10,000 civil fine, a $250,000 criminal fine, and full reimbursement for the Coast Guard's efforts. Authorities are urging anyone with information regarding the identity of the caller to contact the regional command center at 410-576-2525 or email investigators at CGIS-Baltimore@uscg.mil.

In the final stretch of the presidential election, the Obama campaign is leveraging internet culture to drive voter turnout in the crucial battleground state of Ohio. The campaign released a mailer modeled after online cat memes, featuring a picture of a cat peeking out from behind a laptop decorated with Ohio-themed Obama campaign stickers. The mailer urges supporters to "STOP LOOKING AT CATS ONLINE AND GO VOTE" to encourage early voting. This initiative gained significant attention on the social news site Reddit after a user with the handle MollyBloom11 posted a picture of the mailer last night. Simultaneously, federal campaign finance disclosures reveal a surge of high-profile, last-minute donations from Hollywood celebrities and business leaders for both candidates. Barack Obama received four-figure contributions from actors Jake Gyllenhaal, Amanda Seyfried, Renee Zellweger, and Susan Saint James Ebersol, as well as Disney TV Animation Executive Producer Dan Povenmire and author Anne Rice. In contrast, Mitt Romney’s campaign secured 11th-hour donations from actor Kelsey Grammer, former game show host Bob Barker, and Denver Broncos executive John Elway. Romney also received a $5,000 contribution from Sarah Palin’s SARAH PAC, along with funding from the Heritage Foundation and the Consumer Electronics Association. These contrasting flows of high-dollar contributions highlight the intense fundraising efforts between the two opposing campaigns.

Authorities in Huntington Beach are investigating the shooting deaths of two brothers, 25-year-old Benjamin Ullestad and 22-year-old Brandon Ullestad, who were found dead in their shared apartment in the 15700 block of Taft Lane on Sunday in November. Police received a distressed call at 2:45 p.m., and officers discovered the bodies just before 3 p.m. Investigators recovered a handgun at the scene and are currently working to determine the circumstances surrounding the shooting. While the Orange County Coroner’s Office has confirmed the deaths, officials are not ruling out a murder-suicide or an accident-suicide. Police officials, including Lt. The apartment is located near Marina High School, the baseball fields of Marina Park, and the Helen Murphy Branch Library.

The political controversy surrounding the deadly attack on the U.S. consulate in Benghazi, Libya, centers on a 94-word intelligence summary that became known as "the talking points." These words emerged from a daylong email debate involving more than two dozen intelligence officials, who contested and whittled down available evidence into a bland summary. Notably, the final version omitted any specific reference to al Qaeda, an assessment the administration has since acknowledged was wide of the mark and inaccurate. United Nations Ambassador Susan Rice utilized these specific talking points during Sunday talk shows just days after the attack, a moment that has served as a primary proxy for the broader political showdown. Analysts in Washington are currently examining the political implications and underlying factors behind the situation.

A volcanic eruption in the South Pacific Ocean, approximately 65 kilometers (40 miles) northwest of Tonga's capital, Nuku'alofa, has created a new island. The volcano, which had been quiet for five years before beginning to rumble on December 20, is erupting from two vents: one underwater and another on the uninhabited island of Hunga Ha'apai. The newly formed landmass measures approximately 1.8 kilometers (1.1 miles) by 1.5 kilometers (0.9 miles) and rises about 100 meters (109 yards) above sea level. New Zealand volcanologist Nico Fournier of GNS Science visited the site by boat, describing the "spectacular" birth of the island, which is composed mainly of loose scoria. While the volcano has been belching steam and some ash to a height of about 400 meters, the wet ash has deposited close to the vents to build the landmass. Although the eruption disrupted international air travel for several days, the limited amount of ash has provided some relief to airlines. Experts warn that because the island is made of loose material rather than durable lava, it will likely be eroded by the ocean within a few months once the eruption ceases. The island currently remains unnamed, with naming rights reserved for the King of Tonga. The archipelago is situated on the Pacific "Ring of Fire," a region characterized by frequent seismic activity.

The U.S. Census Bureau reported that 16 million children in America received food stamps in 2014, or about one out of every five kids. This figure surpassed pre-recession levels, when 9 million children, or one in eight, relied on the program before the start of the Great Recession in 2007. The data signals a lopsided economic recovery in which lower-income families are still lagging behind. While the number of food stamp recipients peaked at nearly 48 million during the Great Recession, the 2014 figures reflect significant demographic shifts, including a rise in children living in single-parent homes to 27 percent and a decline in marriage rates. Republicans in Congress have sought to cut back on the Supplemental Nutritional Assistance Program (SNAP) as part of a larger plan to balance the budget. While the House initially voted to slash $39 billion over a decade, President Barack Obama ultimately signed a deal to reduce the budget by $8.7 billion. This final agreement resulted in the elimination of benefits for approximately 850,000 people, with the amount estimated to reduce benefits by about $90 per month for hundreds of thousands of homes.

However, a new technology at a Starbucks location in St. Johns County, Florida, is making drive-thru ordering accessible for customers with disabilities. A viral Facebook video, which has garnered over 4.9 million views, captures 28-year-old Rebecca King using sign language to place an order for two coffees. The interaction was facilitated by a two-way video screen, allowing King to communicate directly with barista Katie Wyble, who has had a passion for sign language since preschool. Wyble quickly signed back, and the duo successfully completed the order. Tiri Fellows, a deaf professor who teaches American Sign Language, praised the technology as a long-overdue solution to the difficulties deaf and hearing-impaired individuals face at drive-thru windows.

A Texas tourist, Diana Fiscal-Gonzalez of Dallas, was sentenced to 15 days in jail, six months of probation, and ordered to pay a $500 fine plus $268 in court costs for illegally removing 40 queen conchs from the waters surrounding Key West, Florida. Apprehended on July 13, 2017, by Officer John Martino following an anonymous tip, Fiscal-Gonzalez was discovered with three containers of queen conchs soaking in a mixture of bleach and water. While she pleaded guilty and claimed she was unaware that removing the mollusks was a crime—stating she intended to give the shells away as gifts—the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission strictly prohibits killing, mutilating, or removing living queen conches from their shells, though collecting empty shells is permitted for recreational use. The queen conch remains a significant cultural symbol in the Florida Keys, where the islands are famously nicknamed the "Conch Republic."

Suzanne Shaw, the second wife of architect Peter Cook, has issued a public apology to supermodel Christie Brinkley for ignoring Brinkley’s warnings regarding Cook’s history of infidelity. In a letter obtained by The Post, Shaw admitted she wrongly vilified Brinkley in 2012, claiming it was the "black cloud of Christie Brinkley’s hate" that tainted her marriage, when in reality, Cook was continuing his "skirt-chasing ways." Shaw acknowledged that while she had privately apologized, a public statement was appropriate given the high-profile nature of their divorce and custody battles. The marriage between Cook and Brinkley imploded in 2007 after Cook had an affair with 18-year-old office clerk Diana Bianchi, leading to a bitter legal battle over the sole custody of their daughter, Sailor. Eight years after that split, Cook has legally separated from Shaw, with sources indicating his infidelities are to blame. Shaw reported leaving their Hamptons home in February after repeatedly catching Cook cheating. While Cook’s lawyer, John J. Fellin, denied allegations of infidelity in the separation agreement, the reports highlight a long-standing feud between Brinkley and Cook. Shaw concluded her letter by thanking Brinkley for her forgiveness and expressing a desire to move on privately.

The 2012 Republican primary is entering a critical elimination round as candidates prepare for a CNBC debate in a Michigan suburb. With the Iowa caucuses less than two months away, the primary objective for the GOP field has shifted from making first impressions to survival, where most candidates are now considered one misstep away from political death. The debate is intended to focus on economic issues, including taxation, jobs, and the deficit, but it is expected to be overshadowed by the "Cain circus." Herman Cain’s viability is under threat as he struggles to move the conversation away from persistent allegations of sexual harassment that have plagued his campaign. While Mitt Romney remains the frontrunner, he faces a growing "anybody-but-Mitt" movement crystallized by critics like George Will and Erick Erickson, and the creation of the "Not Mitt Romney" website. Challengers such as Jon Huntsman are attempting late plays to dent Romney's armor, while Newt Gingrich seeks to break into the top tier by appealing to Cain’s base. Meanwhile, Rick Perry and Michele Bachmann face the daunting task of reclaiming their former status as first-tier candidates. Michigan Republican National Committeeman Saul Anuzis suggested the debates are increasingly about "which people make the mistakes and have the most to lose, not the most to gain." The outcome will determine if the field can pivot back to policy or if the controversy surrounding Cain will dominate the narrative.

Phil Robertson, the founder of Duck Commander and patriarch of A&E’s hit reality series "Duck Dynasty," was filmed in the northern Louisiana backwoods on a camo-painted ATV. During the excursion, Robertson shared his vision of a "pristine earth"—a world where nothing interferes with nature—while preaching a gospel of the outdoors. He described the animals as a glorious gift from God, stating that hunting is part of His plan. The group carried a Barnett model crossbow equipped with a steel-tipped four-blade broadhead arrow and a loaded .22-caliber rifle. Robertson’s brand, which began in 1972 with custom duck-hunting calls, transitioned from manufacturing to becoming the biggest reality-TV hit in cable history, earning the family approximately $200,000 per episode. While the show maintains a "no-cussing" tone and family-friendly skits, Robertson has faced scrutiny for controversial views on modern immorality. Specifically, in an interview with Drew Magary, he expressed views regarding the Nazis, the civil rights era, and homosexuality. Following these remarks, A&E suspended Robertson, leading to demands from Reverend Jackson, the Rainbow PUSH Coalition, and GLAAD to meet with network executives and the CEO of Cracker Barrel to discuss the future of "Duck Dynasty" content and merchandise.

Eight critically endangered black rhinos died in Kenya following a translocation operation from Nairobi and Lake Nakuru national parks to a newly created sanctuary in Tsavo East National Park. The Kenya Ministry of Tourism and Wildlife described the mortality rate as "unprecedented" in more than a decade of such transfers, noting that the operation involved moving a group of 14 rhinos in total. Preliminary investigations point to salt poisoning as the primary cause, as the animals likely became dehydrated and entered a fatal cycle of drinking increasingly salty water while trying to adapt to their new home. The relocation was part of an initiative to start a new population in line with the National Rhino Conservation and Management Strategy, with the goal of boosting the Tsavo East population to 2,000. Conservationists, including Paula Kahumbu of WildlifeDirect and Cathy Dean of Save the Rhino, labeled the event a "complete disaster." In response, the ministry has suspended the moving of other rhinos and is closely monitoring the survivors. Officials have invited an external expert from South Africa to conduct a thorough investigation, with disciplinary action promised if negligence is found. This tragedy highlights the precarious status of the species, as there are fewer than 5,500 black rhinos remaining in the wild, with Kenya’s population standing at approximately 750.

A North Korean soldier defected across the heavily armed land border into South Korea on Saturday at approximately noon, marking the first such incident in nearly two and a half years. According to South Korean military officials, the soldier claimed he shot and killed his platoon and squad commanders while on guard duty before crossing the heavily fortified Demilitarized Zone (DMZ) in the western section of the border. South Korean troops reported hearing six gunshots, confirmed the soldier's desire to defect, and escorted him to a guard post where he is currently in protective custody undergoing interrogation. This rare occurrence is significant as the last recorded land border defection occurred in March 2010, highlighting the extreme difficulty of crossing a zone fortified with approximately one million soldiers and one million land mines. While more than 20,000 North Koreans have reached the South in the last 60 years, the vast majority typically travel through China and Southeast Asia. Experts, including Daniel Pinkston of the International Crisis Group, suggest the event is a "one-off incident" that does not necessarily indicate broader instability, noting that soldiers stationed at the DMZ are typically scrupulously vetted for loyalty to the regime. Following the defection, the South Korean Joint Chiefs of Staff activated a crisis management facility and increased surveillance along the border, while no unusual activity has been reported on the North Korean side of the DMZ.

A geologist and gold mining expert predicted that this year’s summer gold rush could be the best in 20 years, potentially rivaling the significant flood of 1997. Weeks of heavy rain and storm runoff across Northern California have triggered a new gold rush by churning the landscape and shaking gold from the hills. In Jamestown, Tuolumne County, "Miner Gary" Thomas reports that the runoff is moving gold out of old abandoned mines and into creeks and rivers, creating new areas for discovery. The receding waters in areas like Traverse Creek are expected to leave gold deposits accessible for panners as streams and rivers recede to just a few inches deep. This activity is widespread, with floods scouring the banks of the Feather, American, Mokelumne, and Yuba Rivers—waterways where the 49ers once searched for gold. In Oroville, businesses are seeing a surge in customers seeking gold from rearranged riverbeds and the Oroville Dam spillway. While the floods have rearranged the motherlode and created "Eureka" moments, experts emphasize that success requires the right equipment, such as gold pans and sluice boxes, to navigate the newly exposed terrain. Despite the high activity, some experts caution that finding gold remains a matter of luck and persistence.

Deena Murphy and Timothy Sullivan, a North Carolina couple, are suing the production company Big Coat TV and contractor Aaron Fitz Construction over the HGTV series "Love It or List It," claiming the show turned their dream home into a "nightmare." The lawsuit alleges that the production company and contractor engaged in unfair trade practices and shoddy workmanship, resulting in a disastrous renovation. Specifically, the couple claims that while they deposited $140,000 for renovations, only approximately $85,780.50 was actually disbursed to the contractor, with the remaining funds allegedly used by the production company to fund the television series. The plaintiffs contend that the show's principals—designer Hilary Farr, real estate agent David Visentin, and contractor Eric Eremita—acted as "actors or television personalities playing a role for the camera" rather than construction experts. The lawsuit highlights several grievances, including the use of unlicensed architects, the failure to show houses actually for sale by licensed brokers, and specific construction failures such as open ductwork that allowed vermin into the home, unpainted surfaces, and windows painted shut. Murphy and Sullivan state they agreed to participate under the guise of considering a move to a rental property with their teenage foster children, only to find the production prioritized creating a "stage set" over quality construction. Big Coat TV CEO Maria Armstrong has stated that the company intends to "vigorously defend" itself against what it considers false allegations.

Anissa Weier and Morgan Geyser, now 14 years old, are facing adult charges of attempted first-degree intentional homicide for the May 2014 stabbing of their 12-year-old classmate, Payton Leutner. The girls allegedly plotted for months to kill Leutner to appease "Slender Man," a fictional internet boogeyman they believed would harm their families if the act was not completed. Leutner was stabbed 19 times in a Waukesha park following a sleepover party but managed to crawl to a path where she was discovered by a bicyclist. The legal proceedings have seen significant developments regarding the defendants' mental health. Morgan Geyser, who has been diagnosed with early onset schizophrenia, has already entered a plea of not guilty by reason of mental disease or defect (NGI). Anissa Weier, currently held at a West Bend juvenile detention center, recently filed a pleading to change her initial not guilty plea to an NGI plea, with a hearing set for September 9. An NGI plea argues that a mental disease prevented the defendant from appreciating the wrongfulness of their actions. Prosecutors have pressed the judge to inquire whether Weier might go this route, which requires the court to appoint doctors to examine the defendant and report on their condition. Defense attorneys for both girls have also filed motions seeking jurors from outside Waukesha County due to the intense media coverage of the case. Despite efforts to move the cases to juvenile court, a state appeals court ruled in July that the girls must remain in adult court. No trial date has been established.

San Jose police have arrested 90-year-old Anthony Aiello for the alleged murder of his 67-year-old stepdaughter, Karen Navarra. Investigators discovered Navarra's body on September 13 at her San Jose home, where she was found slumped over in a chair clutching a large kitchen knife. A medical examiner determined that Navarra could not have caused the deep lacerations on her neck and head herself, leading detectives to believe the scene was staged to appear as a suicide. The investigation relied heavily on data from Navarra’s Fitbit Alta HR, which recorded a rapid spike in her heart rate before a sudden drop-off to nothing at 3:28 p.m. on September 8. This digital evidence, combined with surveillance footage, contradicted Aiello’s claims. While Aiello told police he only stayed for about 15 minutes to deliver pizza and biscotti and later claimed to have seen Navarra driving with a passenger, surveillance showed his car parked in her driveway from 3:12 p.m. to 3:33 p.m. while no footage showed her car leaving. Additionally, police recovered blood-splattered shirts from Aiello’s garage. Aiello is currently being held without bail and is scheduled to return to court on October 4 for plea proceedings.

A male student from Somers, N.Y., identified as "John Doe," has filed a federal lawsuit against the University of Chicago seeking $175,000 in damages, alleging that the institution fostered a "gender-biased, hostile environment against males." The lawsuit claims the university violated Title IX by providing preferential treatment to female students while subjecting the plaintiff to "fundamentally unfair, arbitrary and capricious" disciplinary procedures. The litigation stems from two instances where "John Doe" was investigated for sexual assault; although a university disciplinary panel cleared him of these charges in 2014, the student alleges the school failed to protect him from retaliation by two women, "Jane Doe" and "Jane Roe." The lawsuit details how "Jane Roe" filed a complaint in the spring of 2014 that was ultimately decided in "John Doe's" favor. Despite this, the plaintiff claims the university's actions—including removing him from a physics lab with "Roe" and failing to stop her from repeating her allegations—demonstrated a "pro-female, anti-male bias" adopted to refute public criticism regarding the school's handling of sexual assault complaints. Furthermore, the suit alleges "John Doe" was placed on an unofficial "Hyde Park List" of alleged predators and that the university refused to let him file a Title IX complaint against "Jane Doe" for harassment while allowing her to file a complaint against him. This case joins a growing trend of litigation by male students at American universities claiming gender discrimination in sexual assault investigations.

White House senior adviser David Plouffe did not mince words on Sunday, branding the reactions of Republican presidential candidates Rick Santorum and Newt Gingrich to President Barack Obama's comments on the Trayvon Martin shooting as "reprehensible," "irresponsible," and "hard to stomach." While President Obama met with the Martin family to offer condolences and call for a full investigation into the shooting of the unarmed 17-year-old by George Zimmerman, Santorum and Gingrich were particularly critical of the president's response. Santorum claimed the president politicized the events, while Gingrich characterized the remarks as "nonsense" during a radio appearance, creating a sharp political divide. Plouffe argued that the candidates were trying to appeal to people's worst instincts rather than bringing people together. In addition to these remarks, Plouffe addressed the administration's stance on the Affordable Care Act, expressing confidence that the Supreme Court will uphold the law. He attributed the law's lack of popularity to "fear mongering" and noted that the individual mandate was originally supported by figures like Gingrich and Romney. Regarding fiscal policy, Plouffe took a tactical stance by labeling the House Republican spending plan the "Romney-Ryan budget," arguing that the plan fails the test of balance by providing tax cuts for the wealthy at the expense of veterans, seniors, and the middle class. Finally, Plouffe declined to confirm whether Obama would support a gay marriage plank in the Democratic platform, stating the party would work through the standard platform process.

The Jacksonville Sheriff’s Office (JSO) has arrested 28-year-old Lee Rodarte, a culinary manager at Bonefish Grill, on charges of murder and tampering with evidence following the death of 21-year-old Savannah Gold. Gold was reported missing last Wednesday after she failed to appear for her shift. Her father, Daniel Gold, received suspicious fraudulent text messages from her phone claiming she was "running away," which the family identified as fake due to unusual spelling. Authorities believe Gold was killed in the parking lot of the restaurant, where she had an on-and-off relationship with Rodarte. Surveillance footage shows Gold entering Rodarte’s vehicle, followed by a possible struggle before he drove away; her car was later found with a slashed tire and her purse still inside. Rodarte reportedly admitted to police that he killed Gold and disposed of her body in a pond near Club Duclay Road. He subsequently led investigators to the site, where the JSO Dive Team recovered the remains. Rodarte became a suspect after discrepancies were found in his story, including 2006 traffic tickets showing he once lived on the same street where the body was found. He is also accused of sending the fraudulent texts to Gold's family and tampering with evidence. Rodarte was taken into custody at a Mandarin restaurant and is currently being held without bond at the Duval County Jail. While the JSO is waiting for the medical examiner to officially identify the remains, they believe the body is Gold's. A memorial is quickly growing at the site where her body was found.

Rihanna has been making headlines in New York, where she was spotted in a revealing daytime look featuring a see-through bra top and matching skirt in SoHo. Her presence in the city was further highlighted by a massive nude billboard in Times Square promoting her new fragrance, Rebelle. Despite management's efforts to urge her to tone down her "wild" behavior, Rihanna is currently at the center of a management struggle at Roc Nation regarding her rebellious lifestyle and her refusal to stay away from Chris Brown. While some sources claimed Jay-Z ordered her into rehab, other reports clarify that the issue is her ongoing relationship with Brown and her frequent vacations. The pair are reportedly "seeing a lot of each other in private," and Brown recently joined Rihanna’s family—including her grandfather Lionel Brathwaite—at Jay-Z’s 40/40 to watch the Miami Heat beat the Celtics. Additionally, Rihanna launched a foul-mouthed tirade against The Sun after they claimed she used a body double for an Armani Jeans commercial. She recently canceled a planned trip to the UK to spend more time with her family and her grandmother, Dolly, who is battling cancer. Due to the potential controversy surrounding her current activities, many predict she will not release a new album this year.

Maroon 5 has been confirmed by multiple sources to perform the halftime show for Super Bowl LIII, which is scheduled for February 3 in Atlanta, Georgia. The announcement follows a period of speculation where the band had been on a "short list" for the prestigious event. Frontman Adam Levine had previously expressed a long-standing interest in the opportunity, telling Howard Stern in 2015 that the group "very actively want to play the Super Bowl." The band has "pretty much accepted" the offer, and their schedule allows for the performance between a New Year's Eve gig in Las Vegas and the start of their Australian tour on February 19. While the NFL has not officially announced the performer, a spokesperson stated that it is tradition to speculate while they continue to work with sponsor Pepsi on plans for the "epic show." This selection follows a year in which Justin Timberlake performed a medley of hits and a tribute to Prince during Super Bowl LII in Minneapolis. While Maroon 5 is stylistically similar to recent performers like Bruno Mars, their recent collaborations with artists like Cardi B and Kendrick Lamar suggest the possibility of guest appearances.

The FBI Rocky Mountain Safe Streets Task Force is seeking the public's help to locate a man accused of robbing a Public Service Credit Union in Centennial. The suspect, described as a white male in his late 20s to early 30s, approximately 6 feet tall with a thin to medium build, allegedly took a taxi from the Bellco Theater in downtown Denver to the credit union located at 6850 S. Yosemite St. During the robbery, the suspect wore a black beanie, dark gray jacket, black shirt with white letters, dark jeans, and dark sunglasses, and was observed in bank photos wearing a white dust mask over his lower face while threatening a teller with a small pistol to obtain an undisclosed amount of money. Following the heist, the suspect had the cabbie drive him from the Denver Tech Center to the Denver International Airport, though authorities do not know if he successfully boarded a plane. Video footage from inside the taxi clearly captures the man's face. Anyone with information regarding the suspect's identity or location is urged to contact Metro Denver Crime Stoppers by calling 720-913-7867 or by texting 274637 with the title DMCS.

In Rome’s Piazza Venezia, a 70-foot (approximately 72-foot) fir tree transported from the Trentino region near the Austrian border has become a public scandal. Nicknamed "Spelacchio" (Mangy), the tree has rapidly decayed, graying and shedding its needles just days after being erected. Experts attribute this deterioration to trauma during transport and the application of concrete over its roots for stabilization. Estimated to have cost Italian taxpayers 48,000 euros ($57,000) including transport and decorations, the tree's pitiful appearance has sparked significant backlash and mockery on social media. A consumer rights group has even petitioned an administrative court to investigate government spending and demand its removal. Beyond the aesthetics, locals have adopted the decaying spruce as a metaphor for broader systemic issues in Rome, such as chronic traffic congestion, inefficient public transport, and slow trash collection. These frustrations are further compounded by the fading public approval of Mayor Virginia Raggi, whose anti-establishment agenda has been marred by allegations of corruption and nepotism. Despite the public outcry, there is currently insufficient time, money, or political will to replace the tree before the holidays.

Brett Kavanaugh emerged from two days of tough questioning before the Senate Judiciary Committee without making obvious missteps that could imperil his confirmation as the justice who will pull the Supreme Court to the right and hand Donald Trump a generational presidential legacy. During the marathon hearing, top lawmakers showed vigor in cross-examining Kavanaugh, portraying him as Trump's man on the court. Democratic senators expressed frustration over "committee confidential" documents, eventually securing the public release of a small sample of records. Senator Cory Booker pressed Kavanaugh on why he would not recuse himself from matters involving the ongoing special counsel probe, to which Kavanaugh argued that doing so would be inconsistent with judicial independence. Kavanaugh also faced intense scrutiny regarding the Mueller investigation; despite Senator Kamala D. Harris citing "reliable information" that he discussed the investigation with attorneys at a firm linked to President Trump’s personal lawyer, Kavanaugh denied any such involvement. On social issues, Kavanaugh declined to state whether the high court’s ruling on same-sex marriage was correctly decided, citing precedent, and advised against calling Roe v. Wade "settled law." He also addressed questions regarding his 2015 dissent in favor of Priests for Life and his judicial philosophy. Chairman Charles E. Grassley (R-Iowa) closed the session by praising Kavanaugh for his “compelling and credible” answers, stating that he is “unquestionably... qualified to serve on the Supreme Court of the United States.”

Starting in 1996, Alexa Internet has been donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive, where the data is added to the Wayback Machine on a daily basis following an embargo period. In a separate narrative, the film "Cartoon Wars Part I" follows a conflict between the characters Cartman and Kyle over the popular cartoon "Family Guy." When the show's creators announce that they will show the image of the prophet Muhammad, the network threatens to ban the episode. Cartman views this as an opportunity to permanently remove "Family Guy" from the air, leading the two boys to embark upon a mad cross-country chase to Hollywood to determine the show's fate, which lies with whichever boy reaches the destination first.

President Barack Obama has significantly increased the number of high-dollar "bundlers" to build a war chest for what is expected to be the costliest presidential election ever. According to an analysis of campaign data released Friday, at least 41 people have raised at least half a million dollars for the president, a jump from 27 in his first report. These bundlers, including notable figures like former Goldman Sachs executive Jon Corzine and Dreamworks Animation CEO Jeffrey Katzenberg, are typically well-connected individuals who pledge to gather tens of thousands of dollars for a candidate. The president's campaign finance report shows he can still pull in major cash despite a stagnant economy, dipping approval ratings, and grumblings among some liberal supporters. Obama and the Democratic National Committee have raised more than $150 million so far for his bid for a second term, far outstripping his Republican rivals. The president regularly brings in more than $1 million in a single evening of fundraising, as supporters donate the legal maximum of $35,800 to his campaign and the Democratic party for the chance to have dinner and a picture with him. However, a striking measure of the chilled relationship between President Obama and Wall Street is evident in the latest reports. While Obama dominates in total fundraising, Mitt Romney has successfully wooed the financial sector, raising significantly more than Obama from major investment banks and hedge funds. For example, employees of Goldman Sachs gave Obama only $45,000 this year compared to $350,000 for Romney.

On Saturday, December 17, 2016, a tragic accident occurred at Penn Park in Whittier, California, when a 100-foot-tall eucalyptus tree toppled onto a wedding party. The incident took place around 4:30 p.m. while the group was taking photographs after the ceremony. The falling tree trapped up to 20 people, transforming the celebration into a chaotic scene of tragedy. The Los Angeles County Fire Department and Whittier Police Department responded to the scene, where firefighters used chainsaws to rescue those trapped under the branches. The accident resulted in the death of the bride's mother and left seven other people injured, including a 4-year-old girl who was hospitalized in critical condition. The Los Angeles County Department of Medical-Examiner Investigator Rudy Molano stated that the identity of the deceased was not yet available at the time of the initial report. City Manager Jeff Collier announced that Penn Park would remain closed to the public indefinitely as authorities investigate the cause of the collapse. While heavy rains were noted earlier in the week, officials have not confirmed the primary cause of the uprooting. The city is currently reviewing the integrity of other towering trees in the park.

Authorities in South Carolina began a search on Wednesday for 46-year-old Jimmy Causey, who is believed to have made his second escape from a maximum-security prison. Causey fled the Lieber Correctional Institution, located approximately 90 miles southeast of Columbia, where he was previously serving a life sentence for the 2004 conviction of holding attorney Jack Swerling and his family at gunpoint. Causey and an associate were apprehended three days later at a motel along Interstate 95 after a pizza delivery driver tipped off authorities upon recognizing the customer. This incident follows a history of security failures at the Broad River Correctional Institution in Columbia, where inmates escaped in 2005 by hiding in a trash truck and using fake heads made of toilet paper to deceive officers during a 7:15 a.m. count. Former prison director Jon Ozmint attributed those past breaches to significant budget cutbacks that eliminated hundreds of security positions, leaving areas like loading docks unmonitored. The escape occurs amid a string of state security issues, including a December escape by 47-year-old Michael Allen Williamson from McCormick Correctional Institution, during which he stabbed a police officer. Separately, the Internet Archive is working to preserve web history through "No More 404" crawls. This effort involves archiving pages as they are created to preserve the version live when a page was written, ensuring that references to archived pages replace broken links or provide companion links to show what was originally intended by the authors.

Lois Lerner, the IRS staffer who headed the division overseeing tax-exempt groups, has been placed on administrative leave following a scandal involving the agency's targeting of conservative organizations. According to congressional aides, Lerner was placed on leave after she declined a request from acting IRS Commissioner Danny Werfel to resign. The controversy intensified after an inspector general revealed that Lerner provided dishonest testimony, claiming she did not know about the targeting until 2012, when the audit showed she had known as early as June 2011. While she initially ordered a change in criteria, staffers later resumed political screening. Lerner first disclosed the extra scrutiny given to "Tea Party" groups almost two weeks ago when answering a planted question at a Washington legal conference. Critics, including Senator Chuck Grassley, have called for her removal, citing her role in the controversy and her involvement in the "bumbling" rollout of the disclosure. In response to the scandal, the IRS announced in an internal memo that 25-year veteran Ken Corbin will take over as the acting director of the tax-exempt organizations division. Additionally, Joseph Grant, Lerner’s boss at the agency, will retire in early June. Lerner is expected to return to Capitol Hill for further questioning as lawmakers seek to restore public confidence in the agency's integrity.

Starting in 1996, Alexa Internet has been donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive, where the data is added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. In a new book by Bob Woodward titled "The Choice," it is revealed that First Lady Hillary Rodham Clinton engaged in imaginary conversations with Eleanor Roosevelt and Mahatma Gandhi as a therapeutic release. According to a report in the Chicago Sun-Times, these sessions were facilitated by Jean Houston, co-director of the Foundation for Mind Research, a group that studies psychic experience and altered and expanded consciousness. While Mrs. Clinton publicly acknowledged her discussions with Roosevelt regarding the role of a first lady, Woodward suggests the White House attempted to keep the sessions secret to avoid political comparisons to Nancy Reagan's use of astrology. Additionally, anthropologist Mary Catherine Bateson participated in these sessions. The book also provides a behind-the-scenes look at the Clintons and the Doles, including Elizabeth Dole's decision to decline certain personal topics. Furthermore, Clinton's book, "It Takes a Village and Other Lessons Children Teach Us," involved her virtually moving into the White House for days to assist with revisions. In unrelated news, weather reports indicate varying conditions across Pennsylvania, with Sunbury (17801) expecting cloudy skies and a low of 24F, while Meadville (16335) faces a 90% chance of snow with a low of 19F.

Surgeons at the Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine in Baltimore have performed the world's most extensive penis transplant, involving the scrotum and part of the abdominal wall, on an American military veteran. The patient, who lost his genitals in an IED blast in Afghanistan several years ago, underwent a highly experimental 14-hour operation last month to address "an unspoken injury of war." Led by Richard Redett, the medical team reconstructed the man's entire pelvic region using tissue from a deceased donor. The procedure involved connecting three arteries, four veins, and two nerves to provide blood flow and sensation to the donated tissue. To minimize the risk of tissue rejection, the patient received a bone marrow infusion from the donor, which allows him to take only one low-dose medication daily. Notably, the transplant intentionally excluded the donor's testicles to avoid ethical concerns regarding future offspring. The patient, whose identity was not released, is recovering well and is expected to leave the hospital this week. Doctors expect him to regain both urinary and sexual functions, with sensation expected to return as nerves regrow. The donation was coordinated by New England Donor Services, and the donor's family expressed pride in the sergeant's service. Johns Hopkins covered the cost of the procedure, which is estimated to cost roughly $800,000, and the hospital plans to perform more of these procedures in the near future.

Melquan Kwame Robinson, a 12-year-old seventh-grader at Morgan Road Middle School and member of the Trinity Elite Titans football team, died on Monday after being electrocuted during football practice at the Fleming Athletic Complex in Augusta, Georgia. According to Richmond County Coroner Mark Bowen, Robinson, a resident of the 3400 block of Applejack Terrace, was participating in practice when he went over a chain-link fence. A live wire touching the fence caused a fatal shock when Robinson grabbed it. He was transported to the Children's Hospital of Georgia, where he was pronounced dead at 9:56 p.m. The incident also injured three other people, including two juveniles and one adult. Two young boys were hospitalized after attempting to pull Robinson from the fence to save him. One of the boys, David Sette, was moved from the ICU to a regular room, though doctors expressed concerns regarding muscle breakdown. Don Clark, a former coach and family friend, praised the courage of the children who tried to help and noted that Robinson was very protective of his siblings. Multiple investigations are underway by the Richmond County Coroner's Office, the sheriff's office, and the city of Augusta. Georgia Power stated that preliminary findings indicate they do not control or maintain the voltage that energized the fences. Football activities at Fleming Park were canceled for the remainder of the week, and a vigil to honor Robinson is scheduled for 6 p.m.

Starting early Monday, Hawaii officially legalized same-sex marriage, establishing the island state as a destination for federal-recognized weddings. Moments after midnight, dozens of couples were expected to begin tying the knot, with six couples among the first to marry. At the Sheraton Waikiki, license agents huddled around four laptops in a conference room adjacent to a 30th-floor lounge to process applications for mass ceremonies. Simultaneously, a group of clergy planned a wedding for an openly gay Unitarian minister at a church near downtown Honolulu. Hawaii's marriage laws allow couples to register for a license and be married the same day, a process particularly conducive for tourists. Wedding planner Keane Akao of Perfectly Planned Hawaii has already received more than two-dozen confirmations since the bill passed last month, actively promoting the island's appeal by offering specialized planning services and showcasing locations like Secret Beach. University of Hawaii economist Sumner La Croix estimates that the influx of same-sex weddings will boost the state's $14 billion tourism industry by $217 million over the next three years. The law was passed during a special legislative session called by Governor Neil Abercrombie following recent U.S. Supreme Court rulings. While some couples, such as Cira Abiseid and Cyrilla Owle, are traveling from states that do not recognize same-sex unions to make their marriages official, others are seeking the "aloha spirit" and the established infrastructure of Hawaii's robust wedding industry.

Trading in all Nasdaq-listed stocks and options was halted for an unprecedented three-hour period on Thursday afternoon due to technical problems and "quote submissions" issues on the bourse, according to Nasdaq OMX Group. The exchange sent out a series of emails alerting investors to the technical glitch, which prompted the New York Stock Exchange to stop trading in all Nasdaq securities at the request of Nasdaq OMX. During the disruption, the Nasdaq Composite index had reached 3631.17, an increase of 31.38 points. The unprecedented meltdown paralyzed a broad swath of markets, causing widespread confusion across Wall Street as officials shared limited information during the outage. The exchange eventually decided to resume trading with approximately 35 minutes remaining before the market close after officials confirmed that banks were prepared to handle the transition. All orders for the affected securities were cancelled back to customers during the period of suspension.

MOTUS manufactures specialized hand grips, such as the D-grip and T-grip, designed to improve the ergonomics of manual tasks involving shovels, scoops, and pushers. Clinically proven to reduce the risk of repetitive strain injuries and low back pain, these accessories address the biomechanical mechanics of heavy lifting. A 1992 case study conducted by Dean Kriellaars, Ph.D., at the University of Manitoba's School of Medical Rehabilitation validated the efficacy of the Power-Grip products in a clinical rehabilitation context. The D-grip mounts mid-way down the handle and gives lifting leverage to the forward hand, while the T-grip mounts at the upper end of the handle to provide pushing and pulling control to the back hand. Adding the D-grip moves the fulcrum to the lower hand, transferring the effort to the long end of the shovel and making it a first-class lever. A straight line of force from the shoulder to the grip opens the space between the shoulders for good posture and reduced risk of back and muscle strain. The amount of ulnar deviation is limited to a maximum of 10 degrees as compared to 35 degrees without the use of the straight grip, which is beneficial to the structures in and about the wrist. The use of the straight grip represents a substitution of muscles required to perform the task.

Idina Menzel and Taye Diggs have jointly decided to separate after 10 years of marriage, as confirmed by representatives to PEOPLE. Both 42 years old, the couple first met as costars in the original 1995 Broadway production of *Rent* and later reprised their roles in the 2005 film adaptation. They married in 2003 and have a four-year-old son, Walker, who was born in September 2009. The separation comes despite recent public appearances; as recently as October, Diggs told reporters they were "doing good" at the Men's Health 25th Anniversary Party in New York City. Menzel, a Tony Award winner for *Wicked* who is currently voicing a character in *Frozen*, previously spoke about the complexities of their marriage, noting that they worked through tough times to maintain their connection. Diggs is currently starring in *The Best Man Holiday*. Representatives for the pair have requested that the public respect their privacy during this transition, stating that their primary focus is their son.

New archaeological tests have confirmed that the site many Christians believe to be the tomb of Jesus Christ dates back 1,700 years to A.D. 325, the same era when the Romans first identified the place as holy. To date the tomb, known as the Holy Edicule, conservators from the National Technical University of Athens analyzed radioactive elements in the architectural glue that fits it together. The results, reported by National Geographic, confirm that the remains of a limestone cave enshrined within the church are remnants of the tomb located by the ancient Romans. Mortar samples sampled from between the original limestone surface of the tomb and a marble slab that covers it were dated to around A.D. 345, while additional samples from the southern wall were dated to 335 and 1570, and samples from the tomb entrance were dated to the 11th century. While it is archaeologically impossible to say that the tomb is the specific burial site of Jesus of Nazareth, who according to New Testament accounts was crucified in Jerusalem in 30 or 33, the new dating results put the original construction of today's tomb complex securely in the time of Constantine, Rome's first Christian emperor. The tomb was opened for the first time in centuries in October 2016, when the shrine underwent a significant restoration by an interdisciplinary team.

Billionaire collector and erstwhile casino magnate Steve Wynn’s plan to sell a pair of Picasso paintings for a high estimate of $105 million was derailed after the more expensive of the two was damaged on Friday. Christie’s confirmed that Picasso’s 1943 self-portrait "Le Marin," which measures a little more than 4-by-2 1/2 feet and was estimated at $70 million, was pulled from the block after being damaged during the final stages of preparation. On Monday, Picasso’s 1964 portrait of a woman with a cat, "Femme au chat assise dans un fauteuil," was also pulled from the auction by mutual agreement with the seller. The "Le Marin" was part of a three-piece consignment from Wynn totaling as much as $135 million. The withdrawal of these pieces nullifies the guarantees previously provided by Christie’s and third-party backers, though consignment contracts include insurance provisions for such contingencies. Wynn, who resigned as CEO of Wynn Resorts Ltd. in February, famously damaged another Picasso, "Le Reve," in 2006; that piece was later sold to hedge fund titan Steve Cohen for $155 million in 2013. Despite these withdrawals, Wynn’s 1963 Andy Warhol painting "Double Elvis [Ferus Type]," estimated at over $30 million, remains scheduled for the postwar and contemporary evening sale on May 17.

In response to the 2018 Camp Fire in Chico, California—the deadliest and most destructive wildfire in state history, which killed at least 85 people and destroyed over 13,000 homes across 153,000 acres—Sierra Nevada launched a massive collaborative fundraising effort. Sierra Nevada issued a "bat signal" to the brewing industry, resulting in an unprecedented response where more than 1,400 breweries nationwide signed up to brew the IPA for free. Suppliers have donated ingredients, while wholesalers and retailers have committed to carrying the beer and donating every dollar received. The project aims to produce more than 17,000 barrels (4.2 million pints) of the beer, with a goal to raise over $10 million for Butte County. The Resilience IPA will be available in cans, packaged by Sierra Nevada, and on draught at participating breweries starting in late December. A "Resilience Night" is scheduled for December 20th to celebrate the collective effort. Additionally, the app Untappd will offer a limited edition badge starting December 15th for users who check in any participating Resilience IPA. Homebrewers can also participate using a provided recipe.

Recent research and public health reports highlight significant risks stemming from environmental, policy, and biological factors. A major study found that mothers living within one kilometer of a fracking site were 25 percent more likely to have a child born at low birth weight, increasing risks of asthma and ADHD. Additionally, babies born to fathers aged under 25 or over 51 face a higher risk of developing autism. In terms of public health policy, a landmark study linked Tory-led austerity to 120,000 deaths, while the scrapping of nursing bursaries in England led to 700 fewer trainees in the first year. The WHO has warned of spreading measles outbreaks across Europe as parents shun vaccinations. Legal and medical developments show that the "fundamental right to health" may be jeopardized after Brexit, potentially favoring tobacco and alcohol companies in court. Medical research suggests that cannabis extracts like CBD could provide a "new class of treatment" for psychosis, contrasting with the paranoia-inducing effects of THC. Other findings clarify that common side effects of statin medications, such as muscle pain, are not caused by the drugs themselves. Furthermore, common rice-cooking methods can expose consumers to arsenic, and children's breakfast cereals have maintained high sugar levels since 1992. For mental health, playing Tetris after a traumatic incident may reduce intrusive memories, while chronic sleep deprivation can cause the brain to "eat itself." Finally, Tarek El Moussa, the HGTV star, is in remission from thyroid cancer after a fan noticed a lump on his neck.

Samuel Patten, 47, pleaded guilty in federal court in Washington to violating the Foreign Agents Registration Act by failing to register as a foreign agent for lobbying and consulting work performed in the United States and Ukraine. A former associate of Paul Manafort and a former State Department official, Patten admitted to providing services for a prominent Ukrainian oligarch, Sergei Lyovochkin, and his Opposition Bloc party starting in 2014. His company, Begemot Ventures International Ltd., received over $1 million for these services. Patten’s plea agreement includes a suggestion that the Trump Inaugural Committee improperly accepted funds from a foreigner, as Patten admitted to helping a Ukrainian oligarch purchase an inauguration ticket through a straw-man donor. His cooperation provides Special Counsel Robert Mueller’s office with critical insights into the international political operations of Manafort and his Russian business partners. Patten worked closely with Konstantin Kilimnik, a Russian national with ties to the GRU and a business partner of Manafort, who is due to stand trial in Washington next month on obstruction of justice, money laundering, and failure to register as a lobbyist in Ukraine. Mueller’s office referred the prosecution to U.S. Attorney Jessie Liu in the District of Columbia. Additionally, the Senate Intelligence Committee issued a criminal referral to the Department of Justice based on documents provided by Patten.

South Korean President Park Geun-hye is facing intensifying political pressure and potential impeachment following a massive corruption scandal involving her close confidante, Choi Soon-sil. The scandal centers on allegations that Park allowed Choi to influence government affairs, amass an illicit fortune, and pressure companies to provide millions of dollars to foundations controlled by Choi. Amidst these investigations, tens of thousands of protesters have gathered in Seoul for weeks, demanding Park's resignation as the scandal continues to undermine the country's democracy. In a bizarre development, authorities discovered 364 Viagra and generic pills in the presidential office, a revelation that created a frenzy on the internet and added an air of remoteness to the president. The presidential office confirmed that the pills were purchased in December to potentially treat altitude sickness for presidential aides and employees during official trips to Ethiopia, Uganda, and Kenya, whose capitals are thousands of metres above sea level. Officials stated that the pills were never actually used. Simultaneously, South Korean prosecutors raided the offices of the Samsung Group and the national pension fund to investigate a 2015 merger between Samsung C&T and Cheil Industries, probing whether Samsung bribed Choi to secure state approval. Authorities believe Park was collusively involved in the criminal activities of Choi and two presidential aides who allegedly bullied corporations into making large donations.

Search crews in the southern Indian Ocean have failed to relocate faint underwater sounds consistent with the black box recorders of Malaysia Airlines Flight MH370, which vanished on March 8 with 239 people on board. Angus Houston, the head of the Australian search operation, reported that the Ocean Shield's towed pinger locator detected two distinct, long-lasting signals over the weekend—one lasting over two hours and another for 13 minutes—which were considered the most promising leads in the month-long hunt. However, search crews have been unable to detect any further signals since those initial pings, creating an urgent situation as the locator beacons' batteries are reaching the end of their 30-day expected life. The search covers a 30,000-square-mile zone at depths of approximately 2.8 miles. While the signals were recorded at 33.3 kilohertz—slightly different from the standard 37.5 kilohertz—experts noted that frequencies can drift. Houston emphasized that finding the signals again is vital to narrowing the search area before deploying the Bluefin 21 autonomous sub, which "crawls" along the seafloor and would take many days to cover the vast area. If a sub is deployed to chart the debris field, the crew will replace the sonar system with a camera unit to photograph any wreckage. To date, 133 aerial missions have yielded no surface debris, leaving the fate of the Boeing 777's recorders as the primary key to understanding the aircraft's disappearance.

Kate Vershov Downing, a member of the Palo Alto Planning and Transportation Commission and a founding member of the advocacy group Palo Alto Forward, has publicly resigned from her position and announced her family's move to Santa Cruz. In her resignation letter, Downing stated that after many years of trying to make it work, she and her husband—a software engineer at Palantir—can no longer see a way to stay in Palo Alto and raise a family due to "astronomical" housing costs. She highlighted the disparity by noting that while they currently rent a home for $6,200 a month, purchasing a similar home would cost $2.7 million with monthly payments of $12,177 in mortgage, taxes, and insurance. Downing criticized the City Council for ignoring the majority of residents and charting a 15-year development course that continues the job-housing imbalance by prioritizing more offices and nominal housing. She argued that the city's slow-growth policies and failure to address the housing crisis have put the region beyond the reach of many, including first responders, teachers, and service workers. She specifically called for the Council to prioritize housing by allowing multi-family developments, legalizing duplexes, enforcing minimum density requirements, and allowing housing on top of retail centers.

The Duke and Duchess of Cambridge have welcomed a three-month-old male cocker spaniel puppy into their family. The adorable new arrival is the son of Kate's mother's dog, Ella, and comes from a litter bred by Carole Middleton, who adores the breed. While the couple is happy to confirm the new addition, they have given palace aides strict instructions not to publicly reveal the puppy's name, arguing that it is a "private" matter. The new pet serves as a replacement for Prince William's late black labrador, Widgeon, who passed away approximately two years ago. The acquisition follows a long royal tradition of maintaining family dogs, with records dating back to the 17th century and the historical introduction of the corgi breed by King George VI in 1933. The couple acquired the puppy in early December and are currently settling him into their rented farmhouse in North Wales. The arrival is particularly timely as William is scheduled to depart for a six-week routine deployment to the Falkland Islands as a Search and Rescue pilot; during his absence, Kate will remain in London at Kensington Palace with the puppy. Although the dog's name remains a mystery, the cocker spaniel has been spotted on beaches near the couple's Anglesey home.

AMC Theatres has officially scrapped a controversial proposal to allow moviegoers to text during films following swift and overwhelmingly negative public backlash. CEO Adam Aron, who took the helm of AMC Entertainment Holdings in January 2016, originally suggested making certain auditoriums "more texting friendly" in a bid to attract millennials. Aron argued that demanding 22-year-olds turn off their phones was unrealistic, but the idea sparked immediate outcry on social media, with many patrons threatening to boycott the chain. In a statement released on Friday, April 15, 2016, Aron confirmed the plan was "relegated to the cutting room floor," stating that no texting will be allowed in any AMC auditoriums in the foreseeable future. This reversal aligns with broader public sentiment, as a Pew Research Center survey found that 95% of Americans believe using smartphones in theaters is inappropriate. To remain competitive and navigate shifting demographics, Aron has pursued other strategies, including the acquisition of Carmike Cinemas to propel AMC to become the world's top movie theater chain. Additionally, Aron discussed plans to invest over $1 billion to enhance theater systems and infrastructure, improve food offerings beyond standard snacks, and explore potential deals with The Screening Room to offer home-viewing options while maintaining the importance of theatrical windows.

A Nevada parole board unanimously decided to release O.J. Simpson after the former NFL star and Heisman Trophy winner apologized for his role in a 2007 armed robbery and kidnapping in Las Vegas. During the hearing at the Lovelock Correctional Center, Simpson expressed regret, stating he was misled by associates while attempting to reclaim his sports memorabilia. He maintained that he had been a model prisoner with no disciplinary infractions, highlighting his completion of "Alternative to Violence" courses as a significant part of his time behind bars. The board’s decision was supported by testimony from Simpson's oldest daughter, Arnelle Simpson, who described him as her "best friend and my rock," and robbery victim Bruce Fromong, who testified that he had forgiven Simpson and advocated for his release. Board member Tony Corda noted that Simpson was graded as a "low risk to reoffend." The hearing garnered wall-to-wall coverage, evoking memories of the 1995 "trial of the century" where Simpson was acquitted of the murders of Nicole Brown Simpson and Ronald Goldman. Despite the 22-year gap since that acquittal, the themes of criminal justice, race, and celebrity remain culturally resonant. Following his expected release in October, Simpson plans to move to his home in Florida.

Ardent Donald Trump supporter and actor Scott Baio filed a police report alleging that he was physically attacked by Nancy Mack, the wife of Red Hot Chili Peppers drummer Chad Smith, during a function in Thousand Oaks, California. The incident occurred on a Saturday at an event attended by the children of both families. According to Baio, the confrontation began when Mack berated and cursed him for supporting the president-elect, repeatedly screaming Trump’s infamous "grab 'em by the p----" comment. Baio claimed that despite his requests to calm down for the sake of the children present, Mack remained undeterred and the argument turned physical when she allegedly grabbed, shook, and pushed him. The Ventura County Sheriff's Department is currently investigating the incident and eyeing Mack as a battery suspect. While Baio describes the encounter as a physical assault, sources close to Mack refute the accusation, claiming she was merely trying to show Baio how Trump "hugs" women. The incident highlights a stark political divide; while Baio remains a vocal Hollywood figure to openly champion the Republican president-elect, Chad Smith has publicly criticized Trump supporters, labeling them "uneducated whites" and calling Trump a "sexual predator."

Jim Jordan, a powerful Republican congressman from Ohio, is being accused by former wrestlers he coached more than two decades ago at Ohio State University of failing to stop the team doctor, Richard Strauss, from molesting them and other students. Strauss, who died in 2005, abused team members when he was the team doctor from the mid-1970s to late 1990s. Jordan, who served as an assistant wrestling coach at the university from 1986 to 1994, has repeatedly denied any knowledge of the abuse, stating he had not heard of any type of abuse at all. However, three former wrestlers told NBC News that it was common knowledge that Strauss showered regularly with students and inappropriately touched them during appointments, making it impossible for Jordan to be unaware. Shawn Dailey, who was groped by Strauss in the mid-1990s, stated he was too embarrassed to report the abuse directly at the time, but Jordan participated in conversations where Strauss' abuse of many other team members came up.
