Dailey recalled Jordan saying, “If he tried that with me, I would kill him.” Dunyasha Yetts, who wrestled at Ohio State in 1993 and 1994, also told Jordan about Strauss, noting that Jordan once spoke about his aspirations to become president. The university launched an investigation in April into Strauss after former wrestler Mike DiSabato came forward. While Jordan continues to deny the allegations, the law firm appointed as legal counsel to Ohio State confirmed that investigators had previously contacted the congressman.

Around 4 a.m. on Saturday, January 14, 2017, Johnston County 911 Shift Supervisor Tim Medlin received a cell phone call from a woman who reported being kidnapped from Raleigh and locked inside the trunk of a moving car by her jealous boyfriend. The woman was being transported east on I-40 toward Fayetteville. Although the call was disconnected, Medlin used an office flip phone to text the woman, who responded that she needed help and identified the vehicle as a red Mustang. With the information provided by the woman and assistance from Verizon Wireless to track her location, authorities were able to intercept the vehicle. The woman was rescued unharmed, and her captor, 32-year-old Nicholas Mattevi, was taken into custody and charged with misdemeanor false imprisonment. Johnston County 911 Director Jason Barbour praised Medlin’s quick thinking, noting that while 911 centers can currently receive texts, they cannot initiate them from primary systems. Barbour indicated plans to upgrade the center's technology to allow dispatchers to initiate text conversations directly from their systems to improve emergency response capabilities.

The United States is transitioning to a chip-enabled credit card system to combat a trend where card fraud has more than doubled over the last 15 years, a period during which much of the rest of the world has already adopted chip technology. The new system, which utilizes EMV (Europay, MasterCard, and Visa) microchips, requires consumers to insert their cards into a reader rather than swiping them at a checkout terminal. These microchips function like mini-computers, generating a unique code for every transaction and are expected to reduce counterfeit fraud by 60 to 70 percent compared to traditional magnetic strips. MasterCard and Visa have established new rules and agreements with retailers and banks to implement these changes. A critical shift in liability occurs on October 1, when merchants who fail to upgrade their payment terminals to accommodate "dip" transactions will become responsible for fraudulent charges; American Express will implement a similar liability shift on October 16. While federal law ensures consumers remain protected by bank reimbursements, the new system introduces a downside: processing times will increase from three to five seconds to five to ten seconds, which may cause delays during high-traffic periods like Black Friday. Most customers at major banks such as JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America will receive their new cards by the end of the year, though some may not receive them until 2016. Existing magnetic strip cards will remain functional until they expire, but users are encouraged to use the chip device whenever possible.

Columbia Pictures, the studio behind "The New Adventures of Pippi Longstocking," has taken legal action against multiple websites using images from the 1988 movie to promote a pornographic sex tape involving a 14-year-old girl. Lawyers for the studio issued a formal warning and email demanding the immediate removal of shots of the freckle-faced red head girl, threatening a major lawsuit. While the websites complied by removing images of the DVD and purchase links, many sites continue to host Pippi references and a screenshot from the original movie. The studio issued a disclaimer stating the pornographic material is not affiliated with or sponsored by the studio, even as the related websites maintain a high level of popularity, averaging over 200,000 views per day, primarily from Sweden. Simultaneously, Tami Erin, the actress who portrayed Pippi Longstocking, was arrested on Saturday afternoon in Los Angeles for felony hit and run and driving under the influence (DUI). Erin allegedly struck three separate cars in a series of incidents, resulting in at least one person being hospitalized. During the arrest, Erin reportedly appeared "totally out of control" and refused a blood alcohol test. She has since been released from jail on $100,000 bail. These legal troubles coincide with her recent appearance in the controversial sex tape.

On a warm spring night in 2011, Travis Hughes, a student at Marshall University in West Virginia, sustained injuries after a bottle rocket incident at the Alpha Tau Omega fraternity house. This event, and others like it, have moved from private disciplinary hearings into the public eye through civil lawsuits, highlighting the "forbidding truths" of fraternity life. Historically, the fraternity system dates back to 1825 at Union College, where a secret club called the Kappa Alpha Society was formed as a creative act of rebellion. Today, the powerful political-action committee FratPAC successfully advocates for "freedom-of-association" language in federal legislation to prevent public universities from banning fraternities. The tension between student safety and fraternity autonomy is exemplified by the North-American Interfraternity Conference (NIC), which represents 75 national fraternities. The NIC has actively campaigned to overturn recruitment moratoriums, arguing that recruitment is the "lifeblood" of Greek life. Conversely, critics point to data showing that freshmen account for 40 percent of the 60 fraternity-related deaths recorded nationwide since 2005, with 15 of those occurring during recruitment events. While institutions like Cal Poly and the University of Colorado at Boulder have faced significant rifts or implemented bans due to tragedies—such as the 2008 death of Carson Starkey—the NIC maintains that internal measures, such as banning alcohol at recruitment events, are sufficient to mitigate risks. The ongoing debate reflects a conflict between the desire for unfettered personal freedoms and the responsibility to protect student well-being.

As Hurricane Irene swung north, putting the Washington region in its sights, Maryland and Virginia declared a state of emergency. The Category 3 storm (with some reports indicating it weakened to a Category 2) is forecast to sweep over the Outer Banks of North Carolina overnight Friday before advancing into the Washington area with a vanguard of showers beginning Saturday afternoon. If the hurricane stays on track, the worst of Irene will arrive in Virginia, Maryland, and the District later Saturday and into Sunday morning. The intensity of the storm and the shift in the forecast track farther to the west prompted the decision to delay the dedication of the memorial to the Rev. Martin Luther King Jr. on the National Mall, which was originally scheduled for Sunday. The worst of the weather is likely to be east of the Interstate 95 corridor, where meteorologists forecast four to six inches of rain, prolonged winds of 50 to 70 mph, and gusts of 90 to 100 mph. The exodus from the North Carolina coast has begun as tens of thousands heed warnings to evacuate. Officials and forecasters warned that the storm could threaten as much as $13.9 billion in insured losses and possibly force the evacuation of parts of New York City. In response, New York ordered low-lying hospitals and nursing homes to evacuate, and at least seven states declared emergencies. New Jersey Gov. Bloomberg advised residents to stay out of parks to avoid the danger of downed trees.

Researchers have developed a new process to transform used coffee grounds into a spirit with a 40% ethanol content, comparable to hard liquors like vodka or tequila. According to a study by Nisha Giridharan, the scientists first collected used grounds from a Portuguese coffee roasting company and dried them. The production process involved heating the powder in water at 163°C for 45 minutes, adding sugar, and introducing yeast cells to undergo fermentation. The resulting "Spent Coffee Ground spirit" was then concentrated to achieve its high alcohol content. To evaluate the final product, eight trained taste testers rated the beverage, describing it as having a coffee smell with a bitter and pungent taste. Researchers noted that the flavor profile could improve with age and concluded that the quality was sufficient for consumption. Unlike common caffeine-alcohol mixes, this new spirit is unlikely to contain significant caffeine, as most of the caffeine is removed during the initial brewing process. While microbrewers often use coffee for flavoring, this method represents a distinct way of creating a standalone liquor from waste material, potentially offering a new niche in the global distilled spirits industry. The findings are scheduled for publication in the September issue of the journal LWT - Food Science and Technology.

Pope Francis was “shocked and profoundly saddened” by the murder of four Missionaries of Charity and twelve other people at a home for the elderly in Aden, Yemen. The attack occurred on Friday when gunmen stormed the retirement home, which is run by a charity established by Mother Teresa. Witnesses reported that the attackers moved room to room, handcuffing the victims before shooting them in the head. Reports indicate the gunmen gained access to the residence by pretending to visit their mothers. The four murdered nuns were identified as being from Rwanda, India, and Kenya; they were serving breakfast to approximately 80 residents when the violence occurred. In a condolence message signed by Vatican Secretary of State Cardinal Pietro Parolin, the Pope expressed his spiritual closeness to the families and all affected by this "senseless and diabolical" act of violence. The message stated that the Holy Father "prays that this pointless slaughter will awaken consciences, lead to a change of heart, and inspire all parties to lay down their arms and take up the path of dialogue." While no group has officially claimed responsibility, Yemeni officials have attributed the attack to the Islamic State. The incident occurred amidst a brutal civil war in Yemen that has resulted in over 6,000 deaths and the displacement of 2.4 million people. The Vatican offered its prayerful sympathy and solidarity to the Missionaries of Charity, calling on all parties in the conflict to renounce violence and renew their commitment to the people of Yemen, particularly those most in need.

A Northern California sheriff's official stated that a mother suspected of attacking a 12-year-old boy may have targeted the wrong child. Delia Garcia-Bratcher, 30, of Santa Rosa, was arrested on Saturday on suspicion of inflicting injury on a child after she came to Olivet Elementary Charter School in Santa Rosa on Friday. Around lunchtime, Garcia-Bratcher approached the 320-student campus and asked her son, who also attends the school, to point out the boy she suspected of bullying her 10-year-old daughter. In front of a number of children, Garcia-Bratcher grabbed the boy by the throat, shoved him, and warned him to stop. The boy ran to a classroom and told a teacher, who took him to the office where school officials photographed red marks and visible fingerprints on his neck. While Garcia-Bratcher claimed she was on campus to enroll a third child and address bullying, Sonoma County Sheriff's officials stated they have found no evidence linking the boy to the allegations. Sheriff Mark Essick noted that even if bullying were occurring, the mother's actions were "unreasonable" and "absolutely the wrong thing to do." Garcia-Bratcher was released on $30,000 bail and is scheduled to appear in court this Thursday. Piner-Olivet Union School District superintendent Jennie Snyder confirmed the school is investigating the bullying claims and drafting a letter to parents regarding the breach of campus safety procedures.

Researchers at Amboseli National Park in Kenya have discovered that African elephants can differentiate between human languages and identify specific types of speakers to assess potential threats. According to a report in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, scientists played recordings of the same phrase—"Look, look over there, a group of elephants is coming"—to observe the animals' reactions. The elephants reacted defensively, retreating and gathering in a bunch, by a two-to-one margin to the voices of Maasai men, who are known to occasionally kill elephants in confrontations over water and grazing space. In contrast, the elephants showed no such alarm when hearing the voices of Kamba men, female Maasai speakers, or young boys. Co-authors Graeme Shannon and Karen McComb noted that this ability to discriminate between languages suggests elephants have developed a rich knowledge of the humans who share their habitat. The study highlights that elephants with older matriarchs were better at assessing these threats, demonstrating advanced intelligence and cognitive flexibility. While these findings showcase the remarkable memory and brain capacity of the species, experts also expressed concern that this behavior reflects a growing conflict between humans and wildlife, underscoring the increasing necessity for conservation efforts as humans become a primary "species to watch out for."

Actor Russell Crowe walked out of a BBC Radio 4 "Front Row" interview at the Dorchester Hotel in London on May 12, 2010, after being accused of giving his portrayal of Robin Hood an Irish accent. The 46-year-old New Zealand-born star, who lives in Australia, reacted sharply to interviewer Mark Lawson’s suggestion that there were "hints" of Irish in the hero's voice. Crowe, already facing criticism for his performance in Ridley Scott’s "Robin Hood," told Lawson he had "dead ears" and used expletives before abruptly ending the conversation and walking out while waving his cigarette. Crowe defended his work by explaining that he studied tapes of Michael Parkinson to capture a native English accent, noting that historians link Robin Hood to Yorkshire and Nottingham. Despite voice coaches aiming for a native accent from Rutland, Crowe’s performance remains a point of contention, with some critics comparing his delivery to an Australian doing an impression of Jim Bowen. Following the outburst, Crowe refused to answer further questions regarding his role in "Gladiator" before departing the studio. The incident adds to the actor's reputation for a volatile temper, which previously led to a 2005 charge of second-degree assault in New York.

A Brooklyn grand jury has indicted rookie probationary officer Peter Liang on multiple charges, including second-degree manslaughter, for the fatal shooting of 28-year-old unarmed Black man Akai Gurley. The incident occurred on November 20, 2014, in the darkened stairwell of the Pink Houses in East New York. Liang, who had been on the force for less than 18 months, was conducting a vertical patrol when he discharged a single 9-mm Glock bullet from a landing above the seventh floor, striking Gurley in the chest. The prosecution's charges include reckless endangerment, second-degree assault, and official misconduct. While police Commissioner Bill Bratton characterized the shooting as an "accidental discharge," the indictment reflects the gravity of the event, with Liang facing a potential prison sentence of up to 15 years. The District Attorney’s office also obtained indictments against three other officers for separate alleged assaults. The case has drawn significant attention as a high-profile shooting of an unarmed Black man in New York, amidst ongoing protests over previous cases involving white officers. Gurley’s domestic partner, Kimberly Ballinger, and his mother, Sylvia Palmer, expressed relief and praised the District Attorney for seeking justice. Conversely, police unions have called for due process, citing the dangers of the housing project. Liang’s lawyer decided not to allow him to testify before the grand jury, and he is expected to turn himself in for an arraignment in Brooklyn Supreme Court before Justice Danny Chun.

Authorities in Duluth, Minnesota, have recovered the bodies of Ron Tarnowski, 82, and Mary Tarnowski, 78, who were reported missing on July 29. The couple, who lived a secluded life due to Mary’s right-sided paralysis and Ron’s early-stage dementia, were found on August 5 by a U.S. Border Patrol helicopter in a swampy area near Brookston, Minnesota, approximately 30 miles from their home. They were last seen on surveillance footage at a local store on the day they disappeared, leaving behind their medications and Mary’s wheelchair. Investigators believe Ron, an Air Force veteran and retired firefighter, drove the couple's Chevy Tahoe onto a swampy trail, likely intended for winter snowmobiles, where the vehicle became stuck. Mary was found inside the vehicle, while Ron was found nearby, having apparently attempted to seek help. While autopsy results are pending to confirm the cause of death, officials and family members believe the couple died of exposure, dehydration, and heat on a day with temperatures in the upper 80s. No foul play is suspected.

Pakistani television host Aamir Liaquat Hussain has sparked intense controversy by giving away abandoned infants to childless couples during live broadcasts of his marathon Ramadan show, Aman Ramazan. The program, which airs for seven hours daily, has been dubbed Pakistan's version of "The Price Is Right," where a 500-strong audience receives prizes for answering questions on the Quran. During the show, Hussain presented two infant girls to unsuspecting families, with another baby boy scheduled for distribution in the coming days. The infants were rescued by the Chhipa Welfare Association, an NGO that recovers up to 15 abandoned babies a month from streets and garbage bins. While some viewers praised the initiative as a way to provide homes for "disenfranchised" children, critics have condemned the move as a sensationalist publicity stunt to win high-stakes ratings during the holy month. The controversy is further compounded by Hussain's polarizing reputation as a religious scholar and TV megastar who has faced allegations of using fake degrees and hosting guests who incite violence against minorities. Despite the criticism of the "trophy" style of adoption—noting that Pakistan lacks official adoption laws and requires legal guardianship through family courts—Hussain defends the move as a "symbol of peace" intended to provide a social alternative to the country's issues with terrorism and poverty by ensuring abandoned children are raised as responsible citizens.

U.S. Army Sgt. Adam Holcomb, 30, is the first of eight soldiers to face court-martial for his alleged role in the suicide of 19-year-old Chinese American Private Danny Chen at a remote combat outpost in Afghanistan in October. Prosecutors allege that Holcomb and other soldiers subjected Chen to merciless hazing, including racial slurs, physical abuse, and being pelted with rocks in the weeks leading up to his death. During Wednesday's proceedings, Pvt. Marcus Merritt testified that Holcomb addressed him with racial slurs and threatened to "kill me and put me in a body bag and send me home," stating that he became suicidal after enduring such punishment and humiliation. Testimony revealed that Holcomb bombarded Chen with racial slurs and bloodied Chen’s back by dragging him across rocky ground for disobeying a platoon rule. While Holcomb’s superior, Staff Sgt. Darren Holt, reported the incident as unacceptable physical abuse, defense lawyers argued that Chen’s actions necessitated "corrective training" and claimed that racially tinged nicknames were common "terms of endearment" within the platoon. The defense further suggested Chen’s suicide was linked to his parents disowning him. Holcomb faces charges of negligent homicide, assault, and reckless endangerment, with court-martials for the remaining seven soldiers scheduled for later this year.

Following a massive public outcry and user backlash over changes to its privacy policy and terms of service, Instagram co-founder Kevin Systrom announced that the company is reverting the advertising section to the original version that has been in effect since October 2010. The controversy was sparked by a clause introduced on Monday suggesting that Instagram would share user data—including favorite places, bands, restaurants, and hobbies—with Facebook and advertisers to better target ads. Rather than obtaining permission for advertising products not yet developed, Instagram intends to finalize its plans internally before returning to users to explain the business model. The backlash prompted high-profile users, such as National Geographic, to abandon the platform, while driving a surge of users to rival apps like Pheed and Flickr. Despite this migration, Instagram remains a dominant force with over 100 million members and 5 billion uploaded photos, highlighting the ongoing pressure on Facebook to monetize its massive user base while navigating the volatility of user trust.

New Jersey lawmakers are considering a controversial bill that would impose hefty fines and potential license suspensions on motorists engaged in distracted activities. Sponsored by Assemblyman John Wisniewski (D-Middlesex) and Nicholas Chiaravalloti (D-Hudson), the legislation aims to reduce the 400,000 distracted driving accidents reported in 2014, 3,000 of which were fatal. The proposed law seeks to penalize motorists who multi-task by texting, using computers, eating, or drinking in a manner that interferes with the safe operation of a vehicle. Violators could face fines of $200 to $400 for a first offense, $400 to $600 for a second offense, and $600 to $800 for a third offense, which could also result in a 90-day license suspension and motor vehicle points. While some critics argue the law is an overreach and difficult to enforce, supporters argue it is necessary to break the "multitasking" mindset, comparing the goal to seat belt laws. Assemblyman Wisniewski clarified that the bill does not specifically target coffee or any specific beverage; rather, it is designed to be broad enough to cover any activity unrelated to the actual operation of a motor vehicle to avoid needing new legislation every time technology changes. The bill is modeled after a law in Maine and is intended to educate and save lives by addressing unsafe, careless, and reckless driving.

North Korean figure skaters Ryom Tae-Ok and Kim Ju-Sik secured one of the last qualifying spots for the Pyeongchang Winter Olympics in February after an impressive performance at the Nebelhorn Trophy in Oberstdorf, Germany. The pair, who have been training in Canada and Montreal under coach Bruno Marcotte, performed to the music of The Beatles to secure their qualification. While the International Skating Union confirmed their eligibility, their coach, Kim Hyon-son, stated that the final decision to participate rests with the North Korean Olympic Committee. This development occurs amid escalating geopolitical tensions and war-like threats between North Korea and the U.S., though South Korean President Moon Jae-in expressed hope that their participation could provide an opportunity for inter-Korean peace and reconciliation. The International Olympic Committee is actively supporting the North Korean delegation by providing necessary equipment, travel, and accommodation. Despite the pair appearing relaxed during the competition, they requested that no questions be asked regarding the Olympics during press meetings, emphasizing that the final decision is out of their hands.

Adrianne Haslet-Davis, a professional ballroom dancer who lost her left leg below the knee in the 2013 Boston Marathon bombing, announced on the Hallmark Channel that she is training to participate in this year’s 120th Boston Marathon on April 18. Although she was never a runner prior to the tragedy, she has been working with professional trainers to become comfortable in her prosthetic blade, currently reaching distances of up to 10 miles. To support others in need, she formed the "#AdrianneStrong" team to raise funds for the Oklahoma City-based Limbs for Life Foundation, providing prosthetic limbs to others. Haslet-Davis, who was with her husband when the second explosion occurred, stated she wanted to ensure she was physically and mentally prepared before making the public announcement. In a symbolic display of her recovery and triumph over tragedy, she recently returned to the marathon finish line on Boylston Street to perform a fox-trot. She noted that while her physical recovery is progressing well, her internal recovery remains a continuing journey, and she intends to dedicate the race to everyone who has supported her and other survivors.

The FTC Act empowers the Commission to take law enforcement actions against false or misleading claims regarding a product's origin. Traditionally, the Commission requires that a product advertised as "Made in USA" be "all or virtually all" made in the U.S. Even without an express representation, the overall or net impression conveyed to consumers must be accurate. For a product to be called "Made in USA" without qualifications or limits, the product must be "all or virtually all" made in the U.S., a term that includes the 50 states, the District of Columbia, and the U.S. An unqualified "Made in USA" claim is deceptive if the base is not far enough removed in the manufacturing process from the finished product or if it constitutes a significant part of the final product. For instance, a food processor is not considered "all or virtually all" American-made if the motor is made of imported parts that constitute a significant percentage of the appliance’s total manufacturing cost. The group Truth In Advertising (TINA) recently sent a letter to Walmart CEO Doug McMillon stating that "Walmart’s website is mired in USA labeling errors." Specific examples include Equate store-brand makeup sponges and teeth-whitening strips that were labeled as "Made in the U.S.A." on Walmart.com despite packaging stating they were "Made in China" or imported from outside the country. Walmart responded that manufacturing for these products was recently moved to the U.S.

Nancy Motes, the 37-year-old half-sister of actress Julia Roberts, was found dead in her home on Sunday following a suspected suicide via overdose. Assistant Chief Ed Winter of the Los Angeles County Coroner confirmed the death, noting that Motes left behind an eight-page suicide letter containing personal secrets and "dark secrets" about Roberts. Reports indicate Motes had been battling drug addiction, a struggle Roberts and their mother, Betty Lou Motes, had attempted to address by urging her into rehab multiple times. In an exclusive interview with MailOnline, Conner Dilbeck, the brother of Motes' fiancé John Dilbeck, claimed that Motes deliberately timed her suicide to "scupper" Roberts’ chances of winning an Oscar for her role in *August: Osage County*. Conner alleged that the suicide note was intended to expose "pure cruelty" from Roberts. The tragedy has sparked significant conflict between Motes' devastated fiancé, who is reportedly too distraught to face the public, and her surviving family. Roberts, who is currently on the awards circuit, is reportedly "mortified" and "terrified" by the potential fallout from the contents of the letter. Furthermore, a legal battle is looming over Motes’ estate, as Dilbeck is reportedly seeking to be the sole beneficiary to retain personal belongings, including intimate family photos and diaries.

White House communications director Anthony Scaramucci took a page out of President Trump’s playbook by using Twitter to threaten to sic the FBI and the Justice Department on a suspected information leaker, denouncing the leak as a "felony" and tagging Chief of Staff Reince Priebus. The disclosure, which appeared on Politico’s website, reveals that Scaramucci has accumulated extensive wealth, including assets worth as much as $85 million. According to a financial disclosure filed with the Office of Government Ethics, Scaramucci earned more than $5 million in salary and $4.9 million from his ownership stake in the investment firm SkyBridge Capital between January 1, 2016, and the end of June. Scaramucci owns nearly 44 percent of SkyBridge, which is currently being sold to RON Transatlantic and the Chinese conglomerate HNA Group in a deal valued between $200 million and $230 million. The sale is currently under review by the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United States for national security risks. Despite the ongoing sale, Scaramucci stated his start date would be delayed until August to ensure his holdings are "100 percent totally cleansed." His personal wealth also includes a minority stake in the New York Mets worth up to $5 million and various real estate investments. While Scaramucci continues to be advertised as the firm's managing director, he has been a government employee for more than a month, facing scrutiny over the challenge of extracting himself from potential conflicts posed by his private financial interests.

Hillary Clinton is not considering Bernie Sanders as a running mate for her presidential campaign, according to a Democrat familiar with the decision. Sanders, who has still not officially conceded the Democratic presidential primary to Clinton, was not expecting to be considered as her vice president and does not view the decision as a slight. Instead, Sanders remains committed to changing the Democratic Party rather than "becoming her partner," noting that joining the Clinton ticket would risk him being viewed as a "sell-out" by his loyal supporters. This development follows a private 90-minute meeting between Clinton and a Vermont senator on Tuesday night, during which the specific issue of a running mate was not raised. In a separate development, Alexa Internet has been providing its crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996, with the data added to the Wayback Machine daily following a specified embargo period.

Analyzing data from the National Cancer Institute's SEER Program involving over 258,000 patients between 1998 and 2011, researchers found that approximately 15 percent of cases—37,847 individuals—were diagnosed before age 50. These younger patients are 30 percent more likely to be diagnosed with tumors that have spread to the lymph nodes and 50 percent more likely to have cancer that has metastasized to other organs compared to older patients. The study indicates that these younger patients are often diagnosed only after their cancers start to cause symptoms, such as anemia, bowel bleeding, or a blockage in the colon. Despite these more aggressive diagnoses, younger patients often receive more intensive treatments, including surgery (72% vs. 63%) and radiation (53% vs. 48%), resulting in slightly higher five-year survival rates across all categories: 95.1% for localized disease, 76% for regional disease, and 21.3% for distant metastasis. The findings prompt researchers to question current clinical guidelines that recommend beginning routine screenings at age 50, especially for those with a family history of colon cancer. Medical professionals advise that patients experiencing symptoms like bleeding or changes in bowel habits should seek diagnostic workups, as these signs are often ignored or misattributed to minor issues like hemorrhoids.

The Wisconsin Air National Guard's 115th Fighter Wing is conducting F-16 night training flights in Madison through Thursday to maintain readiness. Residents may see or hear the jets taking off or landing until approximately 10 p.m. Simultaneously, the National Weather Service in Sullivan reported that freezing temperatures on Tuesday night likely caused a "frostquake," also known as a cryoseism, in southern Wisconsin. Meteorologist Justin Schultz confirmed that the boom was felt in areas ranging from Columbus to Fox Lake and Johnson Creek to Oconomowoc. Although a frostquake is a geological rather than a meteorological term, the event occurred when water draining into the ground froze and expanded, placing stress on the surrounding soil and rock. Officials reported that the seismic event, which produced a loud boom and brief rumbling, caused no property damage. Additionally, social media platforms continue to offer features for users to add and manage location information on their posts, including the option to delete location history.

Following a 9.0-magnitude earthquake and 10-meter tsunami on March 11, the Fukushima Dai-ichi nuclear plant crisis has been raised to level 5 on the international scale. TEPCO has confirmed that an external transmission line has been connected to the plant's receiving point to supply electricity, though it remains unclear if the cooling systems in buildings battered by hydrogen explosions survived in usable condition. Engineers are working within a 12-mile evacuation zone to lay 1,480 meters of cable to restart water pumps for reactors No. 2, 3, and 4. Authorities have utilized fire trucks, helicopters, and water cannons to douse the reactors, particularly reactor No. 3, which contains highly toxic plutonium. Significant concerns are focusing on the spent fuel pools; some are dry or nearly empty, posing a risk that the rods could heat up and spew radiation. While some findings were reassuring in the short term, the United States declined to back away from its warning for Americans to stay at least 50 miles from the plant, establishing a larger perimeter than the Japanese government's. To monitor the site, the U.S. deployed Global Hawk drones and U-2 spy planes to provide intelligence for Washington and its allies. The disaster has created a massive humanitarian crisis, with nearly 7,000 confirmed dead, 10,700 missing, and 390,000 people left homeless in northeastern coastal areas. Globally, the crisis caused significant financial market volatility, prompting G7 nations to intervene with billions in currency purchases to stabilize the soaring yen. President Obama ordered a comprehensive review of U.S.

A Knoxville, Tennessee, man was sentenced today to 20 months in prison and three years of supervised release for recruiting individuals to cash fraudulent money orders stolen from a former South Jersey U.S. Postal Service branch. Eugene Bowen admitted to recruiting people, including residents of homeless shelters, to cash the orders for a fee while keeping the remainder of the money for himself and Marc Saunders. Saunders, 40, of Sicklerville, New Jersey, a former employee at the New Lisbon, New Jersey, post office, pleaded guilty to producing the money orders using a stolen imprinting machine. The scheme was made possible when a postmaster failed to return a printing machine during a technology transition in February 2013 and neglected to destroy approximately 180 loose money orders. According to an affidavit by David Bannon, a special agent with the Office of Inspector General at the Postal Service, the machine was stored in a cabinet because it was too large for the postmaster's safe. Investigators discovered the theft in late 2015 after fraudulent orders were cashed in the Philadelphia area; the stolen machine was used to print orders with varying ZIP codes to divert attention from the New Lisbon branch. In addition to Bowen, Anthony Bell, 39, of Philadelphia, was sentenced to a year and a day in prison, and Andre Sutton, 39, received nine months. The court ordered various restitution payments, including $18,470 for Bowen and $22,400 for Bell, while Saunders received a five-year probationary sentence and was ordered to pay nearly $100,000 in restitution.

The idea of a "sober January" has been around as long as people have cursed their in-laws at Christmas, but it is actually a terrible idea rooted in guilt rather than science. While the movement is promoted by the UK's largest alcohol charity, Alcohol Concern, the author—a veteran of many "sober Januaries" who eventually admitted to being an alcoholic—argues that the trend is dangerous for those with real alcohol dependency. Successfully completing a month of abstinence can give the dangerous illusion that one does not have a problem, reinforcing a cycle of denial. The author describes the "all-or-nothing" mentality of "white-knuckling" through January as a common trap for addicts, where the allure of getting obliterated over the holidays followed by a month of forced abstinence is deeply appealing. These concerns contrast with the foundational beliefs of Alcoholics Anonymous, which views alcoholism as a specific physiological allergic reaction requiring long-term self-help. Instead of temporary abstinence, the author advocates for the diagnostic approach proposed by Bill Wilson, co-founder of Alcoholics Anonymous, which involves honest self-assessment through controlled drinking to determine the true nature of one's relationship with alcohol. Ultimately, unless the goal is purely financial, individuals should avoid the "sober January" trend and instead focus on honest self-reflection to determine if they require complete, permanent sobriety.

A rare German Wehrmacht Enigma I cipher machine, used by Nazi Germany during World War II to encrypt military messages, was sold at an auction in Bucharest, Romania, for 45,000 euros ($51,500) to an online bidder. The collector who put the machine up for sale at the Artmark auction house had spotted it at a flea market in the city and bought it for just 100 euros ($114). The 1941 instrument is in almost perfect condition and is part of a historical legacy where the code was famously cracked by Alan Turing and his team at Bletchley Park, a breakthrough credited with shortening the war by at least two years. This sale follows a world auction record set last month by Christie's New York Books, which sold a four-rotor Enigma machine for $547,500. Separately, the University of Winnipeg Library, in collaboration with the University of Manitoba Libraries, the National Centre of Truth and Reconciliation, and the Library and Archives of Canada, has curated and captured a selection of webpages, blogs, news coverage, and PDF files that pertain to Manitoba's ongoing involvement with the Truth and Reconciliation Commission. Supported by Manitoba Tourism, Culture, Heritage, Sport and Consumer Protection, and the Government of Canada, this web-archiving project documents survivors’ stories, apologies, and cultural events to preserve the province's journey toward reconciliation and healing.

Scientists at the Oregon Health & Science University (OHSU) in Beaverton have successfully recovered stem cells from cloned human embryos, achieving a long-standing goal in regenerative medicine. Led by reproductive biology specialist Shoukhrat Mitalipov, the team utilized somatic-cell nuclear transfer (SCNT)—the same process used to create the cloned sheep Dolly—to produce patient-specific embryonic stem cells (ESCs). By integrating a patient's own DNA into a human egg to grow an embryo to the blastocyst stage, researchers can create stable cell lines that are a virtual genetic match to the donor. This breakthrough is significant because it allows for the creation of tissues that can treat illnesses such as Parkinson's disease and diabetes without the risk of transplant rejection. Mitalipov attributed the success to revising a series of technical steps, including the use of an inactivated Sendai virus and an electric jolt to initiate development. The research, published in the journal Cell, involved six embryos created from donated eggs, including two containing DNA from a child with a genetic disorder. While the achievement is hailed as a potential biomedical revolution, experts like Dr. George Daley note that it must be compared against emerging "reprogramming" technology that converts adult cells into stem cells without involving embryos. Furthermore, the breakthrough reignites ethical debates; critics from the National Catholic Bioethics Center oppose the practice as unethical, though Mitalipov clarified that the technique is intended for therapeutic purposes rather than reproductive cloning.

A gang of thieves executed a "Mission Impossible"-style heist on the night of January 29, 2017, stealing over £2 million worth of rare books from a warehouse in Feltham, Middlesex, near Heathrow airport. The items were being stored temporarily in transit for the 50th California International Antiquarian Book Fair. Captured on CCTV, the criminals gained access by climbing onto the roof, cutting holes through reinforced skylights, and abseiling down into the building. The thieves targeted four specific containers, checking the contents against a list and "throwing the ones they didn't want away" before placing the rare books into holdalls and hauling them onto the roof via ropes to be loaded into a getaway van. The stolen collection includes more than 160 rare works, notably a 1566 edition of Copernicus' "De Revolutionibus Orbium Coelestium" valued at approximately £215,000, a 1656 Galileo work, Isaac Newton, Leonardo da Vinci, and a 1506 edition of Dante's "Divine Comedy." The International League of Antiquarian Booksellers has published lists of the stolen items, identifying affected dealers from Italy and Germany. While some speculate the theft was a "to-order" job for a wealthy collector known as "the Astronomer," experts suggest it may have involved inside information. The Metropolitan Police are currently investigating the commercial burglary under crime reference 0502127/17 ABA, and authorities are urging victims to record losses on international databases to make the books unsellable.

Republican presidential candidate Donald Trump lashed out at Amazon founder Jeff Bezos, claiming that Bezos is using his ownership of The Washington Post as a tool to protect Amazon's monopoly and influence corporate tax policy. Speaking with Fox News' Sean Hannity on Thursday, Trump alleged that Bezos is using the newspaper to prevent politicians in Washington from taxing the massive online retailer "like they should be taxed." These accusations followed a disclosure by Post Associate Editor Bob Woodward, who revealed that the newspaper has assigned 20 reporters to investigate Trump's real estate deals in New York, which Woodward described as "more complex than the CIA." Trump further claimed that Bezos is attempting to shield Amazon from antitrust scrutiny by deploying these reporters to investigate every aspect of Trump's life. While Bezos has donated to both Democratic and Republican officials—including a $2,700 donation to Rep. Jason Chaffetz in September—Trump insisted the media coverage is "bad" and "wrong," predicting that the upcoming book and series of articles will contain false information. Neither Bezos nor Amazon provided an immediate comment regarding Trump's claims of political manipulation.

While the subreddit is dedicated to cataloging the naked pictures of high-profile celebrities like Jennifer Lawrence and Kate Upton, it has also become a site for the proliferation of child pornography. Legal representatives for Maroney have issued formal demands to websites like Porn.com to remove the images, citing both her ownership of the copyright and her status as a minor at the time of the photos. Additionally, the leaks included "revenge porn" involving MTV actress Liz Lee, who was 16 when her boyfriend leaked her images. While Apple has denied reports that hackers accessed the images via iCloud vulnerabilities, the situation remains a source of significant legal concern and public outrage. In a bizarre turn, a group of Redditors has engaged in a fundraiser for the Prostate Cancer Foundation in honor of Jennifer Lawrence, raising over $5,273, urging others to "Be the hero TheFappening deserves."

In its third frame, the Disney and Marvel blockbuster "Avengers: Infinity War" earned a huge $61.8 million, combining with a near-record $200 million launch in China to reach $343.1 million worldwide this weekend. This brings the film to a global cume of $1.6 billion, officially making it the fifth-highest-grossing film of all time, surpassing "The Avengers" ($1.519 billion), "Furious 7" ($1.516 billion), "Avengers: Age of Ultron" ($1.405 billion), "Black Panther" ($1.229 billion), and "Star Wars: The Last Jedi" ($1.332 billion). On Saturday, it became the second-fastest film to cross the $500 million mark at the domestic box office. The superhero adventure easily overpowered newcomers during the Mother's Day weekend. In a distant second, the Melissa McCarthy comedy "Life of the Party" earned an estimated $18.5 million from 3,656 locations. The modestly budgeted "Breaking In," a thriller produced by Will Packer for approximately $6 million, took third place with $16.5 million from 2,537 locations. Other notable performances included the comedy "Overboard," which held steady in fourth place with $10 million (a 31% drop), and "A Quiet Place," which remained in fifth place with $6.3 million. Year-to-date box office figures are up 4.9% from 2017, with "Deadpool 2" and "Solo: A Star Wars Story" expected to boost figures in late May.

France is facing a disturbing rise in infanticide cases, characterized by a mental condition known as "pregnancy denial." This phenomenon involves women who fail to acknowledge or accept their pregnancy despite physical changes, leading to a series of high-profile murders. A notable recent case occurred in the northern town of Villiers-au-Tertre, where Dominique Cottrez admitted to hiding her pregnancies from her husband and murdering and burying eight infants in the gardens of two separate homes. This case marks at least the fifth instance of multiple infanticide reported in France since 2003. Other significant cases include Véronique Courjault, who was jailed in Tours for murdering three babies and keeping their bodies in a family freezer, and Céline Lesage, who was convicted of murdering six. Experts, including psychiatrist Pierre Lamothe and gynecologist Michel Delcroix, describe pregnancy denial as a quasi-schizophrenic state where the woman does not view the child as an individual being, but rather as an "inconvenience" to be discarded. While some women may have experienced previous trauma, others suffer from a failure to update their self-identity during pregnancy. Delcroix suggests the rising frequency may be linked to shifting social values regarding family and parenting. Advocates argue that identifying and treating pregnancy denial as a mental illness is more effective than solely punishing the resulting crimes, while warning that many more cases of infanticide may go undiscovered.

John Dean, a connoisseur of scandal, described the internal atmosphere of the Trump White House as a "nightmare" of legal jeopardy and conspiracy. Public scrutiny has intensified regarding the relationship between President Donald Trump and First Lady Melania Trump following a series of viral moments during their international travels. During a visit to Tel Aviv, Israel, on Monday, the couple's interaction became a focal point of media attention when the President reached for Melania’s hand and she appeared to swat it away as they walked away from Air Force One. This incident followed a pattern of awkward public displays, including a "flinch" captured during a Florida campaign rally on February 18, 2017, where Melania appeared startled when her husband touched her arm. These moments have fueled online narratives regarding her unhappiness, with some social media users labeling the reactions as "red flags." While some observers suggest the awkwardness may be a result of the chaotic environment, others interpret it as a deliberate telegraphing of disdain. Sources connected to the first family support claims of her personal dissatisfaction, suggesting her lifestyle was more aligned with Donald Trump's ambitions than her own. Additionally, Melania’s communications director, Stephanie Grisham, addressed a separate issue regarding a liked tweet on Melania's account that mocked her facial expressions, noting that the First Lady was unaware of the interaction until it was brought to her attention.

Tobacco sales and excise in Australia have seen a significant decline, dropping from $5.135 billion in September 1959 to $3.260 billion in December 2015. Data from the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS) and the National Drug Strategy show a steady decrease in smoking prevalence. For adults aged 18 and over, the daily smoking rate fell from 16.1% in 2011-2012 to 14.7% in 2014-15. Among individuals aged 14 and older, the daily smoking rate declined from 16.6% in 2007 to 12.8% in 2013. Significant progress is evident in youth demographics, with smoking among 12-15 year olds reaching its lowest level since 1984; in 2014, only 5% were current smokers. Furthermore, the average age at which 14-24 year olds smoked their first full cigarette increased from 15.4 years in 2010 to 15.9 years in 2013. However, disparities persist: people in remote areas (22%) and those in the lowest socioeconomic status (19.9%) are significantly more likely to smoke daily than those in major cities (11%) or high-SES areas (6.7%). To combat these issues, Australia and other countries, including the United Kingdom, Ireland, and France, have implemented "plain packaging" laws. These laws utilize standardized, murky-colored packaging (notably a drab, dark brown) alongside graphic health warnings to reduce smoking prevalence and increase quit attempts. The Australian Government has committed to reducing the national adult daily smoking rate to 10% and halving the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander adult daily smoking rate, which was 38.9% in 2014-15.

The Chicago Cubs evened the World Series at 1-1 with a 5-1 victory over the Cleveland Indians in Game 2. A central figure in this historic win is 23-year-old Kyle Schwarber, who defied medical expectations by returning from a devastating knee injury sustained on April 7. After being sidelined for six months with torn ligaments, Schwarber transitioned from having no hits in April to hitting .429 in the World Series, recording a double in Game 1 and going 2-for-4 with two RBI singles and a walk in Wednesday’s victory. Cubs leadership, including President of Baseball Operations Theo Epstein, were stunned when medical reports revealed Schwarber was a month ahead of schedule. Following a brief stint in the Arizona Fall League, he was fast-tracked into the starting lineup. Teammates like Kris Bryant and Anthony Rizzo expressed disbelief at his rapid recovery, while manager Joe Maddon noted that Schwarber appears physically capable of running the bases. The victory is being defined by teammates as the "Legend of Kyle Schwarber," a narrative of "utter fantasy" becoming reality on baseball's biggest stage. As the series moves to Wrigley Field for Game 3, the team is evaluating whether Schwarber will play the outfield or be used as a pinch-hitter. Schwarber prepared for the Series by tracking 1,300 pitches from a pitching machine at the Cubs’ spring-training facility in Mesa, Az. Beyond his athletic feat, Schwarber has garnered attention for his personal connection to 10-year-old Campbell Faulkner, a friend with mitochondrial disease whom he supports with a green wristband.

Cassie Young, a 31-year-old digital director, went viral after publicly exposing a personal trainer who attempted to body-shame her during her engagement. After getting engaged to her boyfriend of nine years, Young received a message from an unnamed trainer offering to help her “shape up” for her upcoming wedding. When she politely declined, stating she was already happy with her appearance, the trainer responded by pressuring her to hire him, claiming that wedding photos last for centuries and that she could not be truly happy with her body. Young posted screenshots of the exchange on Facebook and Yahoo Beauty last week, highlighting the trainer's insulting behavior and the "position of power" he used to prey on women's insecurities. Reflecting on her own history of struggling with body image and yo-yo dieting due to past bullying, Young rejected the trainer's superficiality, stating that her self-worth is not defined by her appearance and urging others to live life to the fullest rather than worrying about "belly rolls." Despite the trainer's persistent comments, Young chose not to publicly name him, wanting the interaction to serve as a learning opportunity. The post received overwhelming support from social media users who condemned the trainer's tactics and championed Young's message of body positivity.

The Internet Archive has initiated a "Survey" crawl, starting on February 24, 2018, to archive web pages and their references as they are created. By archiving pages that are referenced, the project ensures that if a linked page is changed or removed, a version of the content as it existed at the time of writing is preserved. The goal is to fix broken links across the web by providing archived versions or companion links to show what was originally intended by the authors. This effort includes crawls of supported "No More 404" sites based on a seed list of 339,249,218 URLs identified in 2017. Separately, a new niche dating website called Mousemingle.com has launched, catering specifically to Disney enthusiasts. Created by Culver City resident Dave Tavres, the site was inspired by a 2011 trip to Disneyland when Tavres realized mainstream dating sites lacked filters for Disney interests. The platform allows users to create profiles and view photos, requiring a $12.55 monthly membership fee for private messaging. Unlike traditional dating sites, Mousemingle.com features specific questions regarding favorite Disney songs, shopping habits, and "Disney nerd levels." Tavres intends for the site to connect fans globally, particularly those living far from major parks like Tokyo Disney or Disneyland Paris, joining a growing trend of niche dating platforms like Whovian Love and Date Vampires.

After the 76ers acquired Julius Erving before the 1976-77 season, the team suffered three NBA Finals eliminations and two Eastern Conference Finals losses in a six-year span. Seeking a turnaround, the team acquired Moses Malone in the summer of 1982 in a landmark deal that was the largest for a player in team sports at the time. Malone, a 6-foot-10 center who made the leap directly from high school to the pros, became a cornerstone of the franchise. In his first season with the Sixers, he won MVP honors by averaging 24.5 points and 15.3 rebounds during a 65-17 regular season. He led the team to the 1983 NBA championship in a 13-game series against the Los Angeles Lakers, where he averaged 26 points and 15.8 rebounds to earn Finals MVP. Over a 19-season career, Malone became the NBA's career leader in offensive rebounds and led the league in rebounds per game for five consecutive seasons from 1980-85. Despite his success, the Sixers eventually traded him and forward Terry Catledge to the Washington Bullets in one of the most-hated deals in Philadelphia sports history. Malone passed away in his sleep in Norfolk, Virginia, at the age of 60. The 76ers organization honors him as a "genuine icon and pillar of the most storied era in the history of Philadelphia 76ers basketball," noting his role in leading the club to its first title since 1967.
